title: A Regression Model

questions.question: What do regression models assume about the relationship between two variables?
questions.answer: Regression models assume a perfectly linear relationship between two variables.

questions.question: What is the goal of using a regression model?
questions.answer: The goal of using a regression model is to identify the signal (true linear relationship) amidst random noise.

questions.question: What do the points in a regression model scatter plot represent?
questions.answer: Points in a regression model scatter plot represent data generated randomly around a true linear line.

questions.question: Why do scatter points in a regression model deviate from the true line?
questions.answer: In a regression model, the scatter points deviate from the true line due to random vertical shifts, either above or below the line.

questions.question: What is the 'true line' in a regression model?
questions.answer: The 'true line' in a regression model is the actual linear relationship that we aim to model but cannot observe directly.

questions.question: What distribution are errors in data drawn from?
questions.answer: Errors in data are drawn at random with replacement from a normal distribution with a mean of 0.

questions.question: How is the vertical coordinate of a point in a scatter plot determined?
questions.answer: In a scatter plot, the vertical coordinate of a point is determined by the height of the true line at that point plus an error value.

questions.question: What does the regression line estimate in a scatter plot?
questions.answer: The regression line is an estimate of the true line that best fits through a scatter plot.

questions.question: What can running a simulation demonstrate regarding the regression line?
questions.answer: Running a simulation can demonstrate how closely a regression line approximates the true line.

questions.question: What can visual representations show regarding the progression from the true line?
questions.answer: Visual representations can show the progression from the true line to the observed scatter plot and the resulting regression line.

questions.question: How can running simulations with different parameters help in regression analysis?
questions.answer: Running simulations with different slopes, intercepts, and sample sizes helps demonstrate how well a regression line estimates a true line.

questions.question: What tends to happen when the sample size is moderately large in regression analysis?
questions.answer: When the sample size is moderately large, the regression line tends to be a good estimate of the true line.

questions.question: What do simulations reveal about the relationship between regression models and the true line?
questions.answer: In reality, we will not see the true line; however, simulations can show that a plausible regression model with a large sample size approximates the true line well.

questions.question: How do a plausible regression model and large sample size affect the approximation of the true line?
questions.answer: A plausible regression model and large sample size contribute to the regression line being a good approximation of the true line.

content_md:
A Regression Model In brief, such models say that the underlying relation between the two variables is perfectly linear; this straight line is the signal that we would like to identify. However, we are not able to see the line clearly. What we see are points that are scattered around the line. In each of the points, the signal has been contaminated by random noise. Our inferential goal, therefore, is to separate the signal from the noise. In greater detail, the regression model specifies that the points in the scatter plot are generated at random as follows.  The relation between $x$ and $y$ is perfectly linear. We cannot see this "true line" but it exists.  The scatter plot is created by taking points on the line and pushing them off the line vertically, either above or below, as follows:  For each $x$, find the corresponding point on the true line (that's the signal), and then generate

 the noise or error.  The errors are drawn at random with replacement from a population of errors that has a normal distribution with mean 0.  Create a point whose horizontal coordinate is $x$ and whose vertical coordinate is "the height of the true line at $x$, plus the error".  Finally, erase the true line from the scatter, and display just the points created. Based on this scatter plot, how should we estimate the true line? The best line that we can put through a scatter plot is the regression line. So the regression line is a natural estimate of the true line. The simulation below shows how close the regression line is to the true line. The first panel shows how the scatter plot is generated from the true line. The second shows the scatter plot that we see. The third shows the regression line through the plot. The fourth shows both the regression line and the true line. To run the si

mulation, call the function drawandcompare with three arguments: the slope of the true line, the intercept of the true line, and the sample size. Run the simulation a few times, with different values for the slope and intercept of the true line, and varying sample sizes. Because all the points are generated according to the model, you will see that the regression line is a good estimate of the true line if the sample size is moderately large. In reality, of course, we will never see the true line. What the simulation shows that if the regression model looks plausible, and if we have a large sample, then the regression line is a good approximation to the true line.

mcq_questions.prompt: What do regression models assume about the relationship between two variables?
choices: Regression models assume a perfectly linear relationship between two variables.
Regression models assume a nonlinear relationship between two variables.
Regression models assume no relationship between two variables.
Regression models assume a quadratic relationship between two variables.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the type of relationship linear regression is designed to capture.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the goal of using a regression model?
choices: The goal of using a regression model is to identify the signal (true linear relationship) amidst random noise.
The goal of using a regression model is to classify data points into different categories.
The goal of using a regression model is to find clusters within the data.
The goal of using a regression model is to increase the sample size to get more accurate results.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the purpose of understanding relationships in data.

mcq_questions.prompt: What do the points in a regression model scatter plot represent?
choices: Points in a regression model scatter plot represent data generated randomly around a true linear line.
Points in a regression model scatter plot are always exactly on the linear line.
Points in a regression model scatter plot represent the actual values minus some fixed error.
Points in a regression model scatter plot represent errors of the model over time.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the relationship between the scatter plot points and the underlying linear model.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why do scatter points in a regression model deviate from the true line?
choices: In a regression model, the scatter points deviate from the true line due to random vertical shifts, either above or below the line.
Scatter points deviate due to errors in the data recording process.
Scatter points deviate because of systematic bias in the model.
Scatter points deviate because the model perfectly fits the dataset.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the impact of non-systematic errors in data.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the 'true line' in a regression model?
choices: The 'true line' in a regression model is the actual linear relationship that we aim to model but cannot observe directly.
The 'true line' in a regression model is the line that fits the sample data points perfectly.
The 'true line' in a regression model is the line that minimizes the sum of the squares of the residuals.
The 'true line' in a regression model is the line determined by the sample with the highest variance.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about what you are trying to estimate but is not directly visible in your data.

mcq_questions.prompt: What distribution are errors in data drawn from?
choices: Errors in data are drawn at random with replacement from a normal distribution with a mean of 0.
Errors in data are drawn from a uniform distribution without replacement.
Errors in data are drawn from a Poisson distribution with a mean greater than 0.
Errors in data arise from a skewed distribution with varying means.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the properties of a distribution often used to model random errors.

mcq_questions.prompt: How is the vertical coordinate of a point in a scatter plot determined?
choices: In a scatter plot, the vertical coordinate of a point is determined by the height of the true line at that point plus an error value.
In a scatter plot, the vertical coordinate of a point is determined by the highest data value on the y-axis.
In a scatter plot, the vertical coordinate of a point is determined by the average of all data points.
In a scatter plot, the vertical coordinate of a point is determined by the slope of the line of best fit.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how deviations from the expected line contribute to the point's position.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the regression line estimate in a scatter plot?
choices: The regression line is an estimate of the true line that best fits through a scatter plot.
The regression line is an estimate of the average value of the data points.
The regression line predicts the correlation between two variables.
The regression line indicates the direction of data point distribution.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how the regression line relates to the overall trend in the data.

mcq_questions.prompt: What can running a simulation demonstrate regarding the regression line?
choices: Running a simulation can demonstrate how closely a regression line approximates the true line.
Running a simulation can determine the exact coefficients of the regression line.
Running a simulation can show which variables are the most important.
Running a simulation can provide the dataset with the highest correlation.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the purpose of comparing simulated results with the expected model.

mcq_questions.prompt: What can visual representations show regarding the progression from the true line?
choices: Visual representations can show the progression from the true line to the observed scatter plot and the resulting regression line.
Visual representations can show only the observed scatter plot without any progression.
Visual representations can only display the resulting regression line purely from assumptions.
Visual representations can indicate data points that are not part of any regression analysis.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how visualizations reveal relationships between true data and observed phenomena.

mcq_questions.prompt: How can running simulations with different parameters help in regression analysis?
choices: Running simulations with different slopes, intercepts, and sample sizes helps demonstrate how well a regression line estimates a true line.
Simulations help eliminate all variability in data, making prediction easier.
By simulating different noise levels, we can confirm the regression line remains the same.
Simulations change the data distribution entirely, rendering regression analysis unnecessary.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how altering elements like slope and sample size can affect the estimation of a regression line.

mcq_questions.prompt: What tends to happen when the sample size is moderately large in regression analysis?
choices: When the sample size is moderately large, the regression line tends to be a good estimate of the true line.
With a moderately large sample size, the regression line becomes highly sensitive to outliers.
A moderately large sample size makes the regression line irrelevant for any predictions.
When the sample size is moderately large, the regression line is likely to show spurious correlations.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how sample size affects the reliability and accuracy of statistical estimates.

mcq_questions.prompt: What do simulations reveal about the relationship between regression models and the true line?
choices: In reality, we will not see the true line; however, simulations can show that a plausible regression model with a large sample size approximates the true line well.
Simulations always provide the exact true line, regardless of the sample size.
The true line is visible in simulations even with very small sample sizes.
Simulations are unable to tell us anything about the true line.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how simulations use large sample sizes to approximate outcomes.

mcq_questions.prompt: How do a plausible regression model and large sample size affect the approximation of the true line?
choices: A plausible regression model and large sample size contribute to the regression line being a good approximation of the true line.
A plausible regression model and large sample size make the regression line weaker and less accurate.
A plausible regression model and large sample size remove the need for any approximation of the true line.
A plausible regression model and large sample size prevent the regression line from approximating the true line at all.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how reliability and sample representativeness affect the accuracy of a model.

title: The Method of Least Squares

questions.question: What does the method of least squares aim to find in a scatter plot?
questions.answer: The method of least squares aims to find the line that minimizes the overall error in predicting the values of y, given x, in a scatter plot.

questions.question: What is a key criterion for a regression line to be considered 'best'?
questions.answer: A key criterion for a regression line to be considered 'best' is that it has the smallest possible total error among all possible straight lines.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a regression line?
questions.answer: The purpose of a regression line is to predict or estimate values of a dependent variable based on the values of an independent variable.

questions.question: Can a scatter plot that isn't perfectly linear still have a 'best' line using the least squares method?
questions.answer: Even if a scatter plot isn't perfectly linear or 'football shaped,' it can still have a 'best' line using the least squares method.

questions.question: Why do errors in predictions occur in a regression model?
questions.answer: Errors in predictions occur because the estimated values of y are not exactly equal to the true values, and the 'best' line minimizes these errors.

questions.question: What is an error of prediction on a scatter plot?
questions.answer: An error of prediction is the difference between the actual value and the predicted value for each point on a scatter plot, measured as the vertical distance.

questions.question: How do you determine the 'best' line for prediction?
questions.answer: Determining the 'best' line requires defining criteria for what 'best' means, which involves minimizing prediction errors.

questions.question: What are the key parameters used to define the equation of a line?
questions.answer: Slope and intercept are key parameters used to define the equation of a line.

questions.question: How are errors in a fitted line model visualized?
questions.answer: Errors in a fitted line model are visualized as vertical distances from the points to the line.

questions.question: What happens if you select different lines for estimation?
questions.answer: Selecting different lines for estimation will result in different errors.

questions.question: What is the function 'lwerrors' used for?
questions.answer: The function 'lwerrors' is used to visualize errors by drawing a figure based on a given slope and intercept.

questions.question: What is mean squared error (MSE) a measure of and why are its units difficult to interpret?
questions.answer: Mean squared error (MSE) is a measure of how large the squared errors are on average, but its units are difficult to interpret.

questions.question: What makes root mean square error (RMSE) easier to understand compared to mean squared error?
questions.answer: Root mean square error (RMSE) is the square root of the mean squared error and is in the same units as the variable being predicted, making it easier to understand.

questions.question: How is the RMSE of a line used in prediction?
questions.answer: The RMSE of a line is used to estimate how close the predictions are to the actual values of the variable being predicted.

questions.question: Why do we use the mean of squared errors instead of the mean of the errors themselves?
questions.answer: To avoid errors canceling each other out, we use the mean of squared errors rather than the mean of the errors themselves.

questions.question: What is the root mean squared error used to evaluate?
questions.answer: The root mean squared error (RMSE) is a measure used to evaluate the accuracy of a line through a scatter diagram.

questions.question: What is the name of the line that minimizes the root mean squared error in linear regression?
questions.answer: In the context of linear regression, the line that minimizes the root mean squared error among all potential lines is called the regression line.

questions.question: What parameters are involved in calculating the RMSE for any line?
questions.answer: The function to calculate the RMSE for any line involves taking the slope and intercept as parameters.

questions.question: What does the regression line uniquely minimize compared to other lines?
questions.answer: The regression line uniquely minimizes the mean squared error of estimation when compared to other lines.

questions.question: How can Python be used in the context of regression lines?
questions.answer: Python can be used to confirm that the regression line minimizes the mean squared error.

questions.question: What does a line that minimizes the root mean squared error also affect?
questions.answer: A line that minimizes the root mean squared error also minimizes the squared error, as the square root does not affect minimization.

questions.question: What can you do to simplify computations related to error minimization?
questions.answer: To simplify computations, you can directly minimize the mean squared error instead of focusing on the root mean squared error.

questions.question: On what factors does the mean squared error of a prediction depend?
questions.answer: The mean squared error (mse) of a prediction depends on the slope and intercept of the line used for prediction.

questions.question: How is the root mean squared error (RMSE) obtained and what does it provide?
questions.answer: The root mean squared error (RMSE) is obtained by taking the square root of the mean squared error, providing a measure in the same units as the original data.

questions.question: What does the minimize function in Python help achieve?
questions.answer: The minimize function in Python helps find the minimum value of a function by adjusting its parameters using a trial-and-error approach.

questions.question: How can we efficiently optimize regression parameters like slope and intercept in Python?
questions.answer: Using Python functions like minimize, we can efficiently optimize regression parameters such as slope and intercept without manually testing different values.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'minimize' function when applied to a given function's output?
questions.answer: The 'minimize' function is used to find values that minimize a given function's output through intelligent trial and error.

questions.question: What does the function 'lwmse' return when given a numerical slope and intercept?
questions.answer: The function 'lwmse' takes a numerical slope and intercept and returns the corresponding mean squared error (MSE).

questions.question: What does the regression line achieve by minimizing the mean squared error (MSE)?
questions.answer: The regression line minimizes the mean squared error (MSE) and is derived as a result of the minimizing process.

questions.question: What might be a limitation of the values found using 'minimize'?
questions.answer: The minimizing values found using 'minimize' are approximations, not exact, and may show slight deviations.

questions.question: Why is the regression line also known as the 'least squares line'?
questions.answer: The regression line is also known as the 'least squares line' because it minimizes mean squared error.

content_md:
The Method of Least Squares We have developed the equation of the regression line that runs through a football shaped scatter plot. But not all scatter plots are football shaped, not even linear ones. Does every scatter plot have a "best" line that goes through it? If so, can we still use the formulas for the slope and intercept developed in the previous section, or do we need new ones? To address these questions, we need a reasonable definition of "best". Recall that the purpose of the line is to predict or estimate values of $y$, given values of $x$. Estimates typically aren't perfect. Each one is off the true value by an error. A reasonable criterion for a line to be the "best" is for it to have the smallest possible overall error among all straight lines. In this section we will make this criterion precise and see if we can identify the best straight line under the criterion. Our fir

st example is a dataset that has one row for every chapter of the novel "Little Women." The goal is to estimate the number of characters (that is, letters, spaces punctuation marks, and so on) based on the number of periods. Recall that we attempted to do this in the very first lecture of this course. To explore the data, we will need to use the functions correlation, slope, intercept, and fit defined in the previous section. The scatter plot is remarkably close to linear, and the correlation is more than 0.92.  Error in Estimation The graph below shows the scatter plot and line that we developed in the previous section. We don't yet know if that's the best among all lines. We first have to say precisely what "best" means. Corresponding to each point on the scatter plot, there is an error of prediction calculated as the actual value minus the predicted value. It is the vertical distance 

between the point and the line, with a negative sign if the point is below the line. We can use slope and intercept to calculate the slope and intercept of the fitted line. The graph below shows the line (in light blue). The errors corresponding to four of the points are shown in red. There is nothing special about those four points. They were just chosen for clarity of the display. The function lwerrors takes a slope and an intercept (in that order) as its arguments and draws the figure. Had we used a different line to create our estimates, the errors would have been different. The graph below shows how big the errors would be if we were to use another line for estimation. The second graph shows large errors obtained by using a line that is downright silly.  Root Mean Squared Error What we need now is one overall measure of the rough size of the errors. You will recognize the approach t

o creating this – it's exactly the way we developed the SD. If you use any arbitrary line to calculate your estimates, then some of your errors are likely to be positive and others negative. To avoid cancellation when measuring the rough size of the errors, we will take the mean of the squared errors rather than the mean of the errors themselves. The mean squared error of estimation is a measure of roughly how big the squared errors are, but as we have noted earlier, its units are hard to interpret. Taking the square root yields the root mean square error (rmse), which is in the same units as the variable being predicted and therefore much easier to understand.  Minimizing the Root Mean Squared Error Our observations so far can be summarized as follows.  To get estimates of $y$ based on $x$, you can use any line you want.  Every line has a root mean squared error of estimation.  "Better"

 lines have smaller errors. Is there a "best" line? That is, is there a line that minimizes the root mean squared error among all lines? To answer this question, we will start by defining a function lwrmse to compute the root mean squared error of any line through the Little Women scatter diagram. The function takes the slope and the intercept (in that order) as its arguments. Bad lines have big values of rmse, as expected. But the rmse is much smaller if we choose a slope and intercept close to those of the regression line. Here is the root mean squared error corresponding to the regression line. By a remarkable fact of mathematics, no other line can beat this one.  The regression line is the unique straight line that minimizes the mean squared error of estimation among all straight lines. The proof of this statement requires abstract mathematics that is beyond the scope of this course.

 On the other hand, we do have a powerful tool – Python – that performs large numerical computations with ease. So we can use Python to confirm that the regression line minimizes the mean squared error.  Numerical Optimization  First note that a line that minimizes the root mean squared error is also a line that minimizes the squared error. The square root makes no difference to the minimization. So we will save ourselves a step of computation and just minimize the mean squared error (mse). We are trying to predict the number of characters ($y$) based on the number of periods ($x$) in chapters of Little Women. If we use the line $$ \mbox{prediction}  ax + b $$ it will have an mse that depends on the slope $a$ and the intercept $b$. The function lwmse takes the slope and intercept as its arguments and returns the corresponding mse. Let's check that lwmse gets the right answer for the root

 mean squared error of the regression line. Remember that lwmse returns the mean squared error, so we have to take the square root to get the rmse. That's the same as the value we got by using lwrmse earlier: You can confirm that lwmse returns the correct value for other slopes and intercepts too. For example, here is the rmse of the extremely bad line that we tried earlier. And here is the rmse for a line that is close to the regression line. If we experiment with different values, we can find a lowerror slope and intercept through trial and error, but that would take a while. Fortunately, there is a Python function that does all the trial and error for us. The minimize function can be used to find the arguments of a function for which the function returns its minimum value. Python uses a similar trialanderror approach, following the changes that lead to incrementally lower output value

s. The argument of minimize is a function that itself takes numerical arguments and returns a numerical value. For example, the function lwmse takes a numerical slope and intercept as its arguments and returns the corresponding mse. The call minimize(lwmse) returns an array consisting of the slope and the intercept that minimize the mse. These minimizing values are excellent approximations arrived at by intelligent trialanderror, not exact values based on formulas. These values are the same as the values we calculated earlier by using the slope and intercept functions. We see small deviations due to the inexact nature of minimize, but the values are essentially the same.  The Least Squares Line Therefore, we have found not only that the regression line minimizes mean squared error, but also that minimizing mean squared error gives us the regression line. The regression line is the only l

ine that minimizes mean squared error. That is why the regression line is sometimes called the "least squares line."

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the method of least squares aim to find in a scatter plot?
choices: The method of least squares aims to find the line that minimizes the overall error in predicting the values of y, given x, in a scatter plot.
The method of least squares identifies the median line that passes through the most data points in a scatter plot.
The method of least squares seeks the line that maximizes the correlation coefficient in a scatter plot.
The method of least squares finds the line that averages the differences between x and y values in a scatter plot.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the goal of reducing the sum of squared differences between observed and predicted values.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is a key criterion for a regression line to be considered 'best'?
choices: A key criterion for a regression line to be considered 'best' is that it has the smallest possible total error among all possible straight lines.
The total error of the regression line must equal zero.
The regression line must pass through all data points.
The slope of the regression line must be positive.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the measure of fitting accuracy for regression lines.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the purpose of a regression line?
choices: The purpose of a regression line is to predict or estimate values of a dependent variable based on the values of an independent variable.
The purpose of a regression line is to identify the maximum values of a data set.
The purpose of a regression line is to calculate the average values of multiple data sets.
The purpose of a regression line is to determine the central tendency of a frequency distribution.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how a regression line relates to prediction and estimation.

mcq_questions.prompt: Can a scatter plot that isn't perfectly linear still have a 'best' line using the least squares method?
choices: Even if a scatter plot isn't perfectly linear or 'football shaped,' it can still have a 'best' line using the least squares method.
A scatter plot needs to be perfectly linear to have a 'best' line with the least squares method.
Only scatter plots with no outliers can have a 'best' line using the least squares method.
If a scatter plot is not parabolic, it cannot have a 'best' line using the least squares method.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the least squares method works with data that is not perfectly aligned.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why do errors in predictions occur in a regression model?
choices: Errors in predictions occur because the estimated values of y are not exactly equal to the true values, and the 'best' line minimizes these errors.
Errors occur because the model always perfectly fits the data without any deviations.
Errors happen because all data points lie exactly on a straight line.
Errors are due to the fact that the regression model uses different variables in every prediction that cause inconsistencies.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how a regression line tries to fit data points as closely as possible but rarely matches them perfectly.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is an error of prediction on a scatter plot?
choices: An error of prediction is the difference between the actual value and the predicted value for each point on a scatter plot, measured as the vertical distance.
An error of prediction is the sum of squared differences between observed and predicted values.
An error of prediction is the horizontal distance between each point and the y-axis.
An error of prediction is the difference between the maximum and minimum values in the dataset.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how we measure deviations between expected and observed values on a graph.

mcq_questions.prompt: How do you determine the 'best' line for prediction?
choices: Determining the 'best' line requires defining criteria for what 'best' means, which involves minimizing prediction errors.
The 'best' line is the one that passes through the most data points.
The 'best' line is the longest possible line one can draw through the graph.
The 'best' line is a horizontal line at the average value of the dataset.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what it means for a line to accurately predict outcomes.

mcq_questions.prompt: What are the key parameters used to define the equation of a line?
choices: Slope and intercept are key parameters used to define the equation of a line.
Length and width are key parameters used to define the equation of a line.
Radius and diameter are key parameters used to define the equation of a line.
Vertices and angles are key parameters used to define the equation of a line.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about linear equations in the form y = mx + b.

mcq_questions.prompt: How are errors in a fitted line model visualized?
choices: Errors in a fitted line model are visualized as vertical distances from the points to the line.
Errors in a fitted line model are visualized as horizontal distances from the line to the points.
Errors in a fitted line model are visualized as the angle between the line and the points.
Errors in a fitted line model are visualized as the color of the points on the graph.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how distances between points and lines are generally measured.

mcq_questions.prompt: What happens if you select different lines for estimation?
choices: Selecting different lines for estimation will result in different errors.
Selecting different lines for estimation will have no effect.
Selecting different lines will produce identical estimations.
Selecting different lines will always give more accurate results.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the choice of lines might impact the variability of errors.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the function 'lwerrors' used for?
choices: The function 'lwerrors' is used to visualize errors by drawing a figure based on a given slope and intercept.
The function 'lwerrors' is used to calculate the mean error of a dataset.
The function 'lwerrors' is used to log errors and store them in a file.
The function 'lwerrors' is used to correct errors in a dataset using linear regression.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about visualization and graphical representation related to 'slope' and 'intercept'.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is mean squared error (MSE) a measure of and why are its units difficult to interpret?
choices: Mean squared error (MSE) is a measure of how large the squared errors are on average, but its units are difficult to interpret.
Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average squared difference between actual and predicted values, and it uses percentages which are hard to understand.
Mean squared error (MSE) quantifies the percentage of errors in predictions, using units that are misleading.
Mean squared error (MSE) assesses the total number of errors without regard to their size, making its interpretation challenging.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what aspect of errors MSE focuses on and the implication of squaring those errors.

mcq_questions.prompt: What makes root mean square error (RMSE) easier to understand compared to mean squared error?
choices: Root mean square error (RMSE) is the square root of the mean squared error and is in the same units as the variable being predicted, making it easier to understand.
Root mean square error (RMSE) provides an exact adjustment to predictions, simplifying interpretation.
Root mean square error (RMSE) maximizes the model's overall performance by overfitting the data.
Root mean square error (RMSE) only captures the variance in the model, which is often more intuitive.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how RMSE relates to the units of the data it predicts.

mcq_questions.prompt: How is the RMSE of a line used in prediction?
choices: The RMSE of a line is used to estimate how close the predictions are to the actual values of the variable being predicted.
The RMSE of a line determines the slope of the line in a linear regression model.
The RMSE of a line indicates the number of variables in the prediction model.
The RMSE of a line calculates the average value of the dataset being predicted.
mcq_questions.hint: RMSE is a metric that measures prediction accuracy.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why do we use the mean of squared errors instead of the mean of the errors themselves?
choices: To avoid errors canceling each other out, we use the mean of squared errors rather than the mean of the errors themselves.
Because squaring the errors provides a more interpretable metric.
To simplify the calculations required for optimization.
To account for the direction of errors and enhance prediction accuracy.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what happens when positive and negative errors are averaged.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the root mean squared error used to evaluate?

content_md:
Least Squares Regression In an earlier section, we developed formulas for the slope and intercept of the regression line through a football shaped scatter diagram. It turns out that the slope and intercept of the least squares line have the same formulas as those we developed, regardless of the shape of the scatter plot. We saw this in the example about Little Women, but let's confirm it in an example where the scatter plot clearly isn't football shaped. For the data, we are once again indebted to the rich  of the University of Florida. A  in the International Journal of Exercise Science studied collegiate shot put athletes and examined the relation between strength and shot put distance. The population consists of 28 female collegiate athletes. Strength was measured by the the biggest amount (in kilograms) that the athlete lifted in the "1RM power clean" in the preseason. The distance (

in meters) was the athlete's personal best. That's not a football shaped scatter plot. In fact, it seems to have a slight nonlinear component. But if we insist on using a straight line to make our predictions, there is still one best straight line among all straight lines. Our formulas for the slope and intercept of the regression line, derived for football shaped scatter plots, give the following values. Does it still make sense to use these formulas even though the scatter plot isn't football shaped? We can answer this by finding the slope and intercept of the line that minimizes the mse. We will define the function shotputlinearmse to take an arbirtary slope and intercept as arguments and return the corresponding mse. Then minimize applied to shotputlinearmse will return the best slope and intercept. These values are the same as those we got by using our formulas. To summarize: No mat

ter what the shape of the scatter plot, there is a unique line that minimizes the mean squared error of estimation. It is called the regression line, and its slope and intercept are given by $$ \mathbf{\mbox{slope of the regression line}}  r \cdot \frac{\mbox{SD of }y}{\mbox{SD of }x} $$ $$ \mathbf{\mbox{intercept of the regression line}}  \mbox{average of }y  \mbox{slope} \cdot \mbox{average of }x $$  Nonlinear Regression The graph above reinforces our earlier observation that the scatter plot is a bit curved. So it is better to fit a curve than a straight line. The  postulated a quadratic relation between the weight lifted and the shot put distance. So let's use quadratic functions as our predictors and see if we can find the best one. We have to find the best quadratic function among all quadratic functions, instead of the best straight line among all straight lines. The method of lea

st squares allows us to do this. The mathematics of this minimization is complicated and not easy to see just by examining the scatter plot. But numerical minimization is just as easy as it was with linear predictors! We can get the best quadratic predictor by once again using minimize. Let's see how this works. Recall that a quadratic function has the form $$ f(x)  ax^2 + bx + c $$ for constants $a$, $b$, and $c$. To find the best quadratic function to predict distance based on weight lifted, using the criterion of least squares, we will first write a function that takes the three constants as its arguments, calculates the fitted values by using the quadratic function above, and then returns the mean squared error. The function is called shotputquadraticmse. Notice that the definition is analogous to that of lwmse, except that the fitted values are based on a quadratic function instead 

of linear. We can now use minimize just as before to find the constants that minimize the mean squared error. Our prediction of the shot put distance for an athlete who lifts $x$ kilograms is about $$ 0.00104x^2 + 0.2827x  1.5318 $$ meters. For example, if the athlete can lift 100 kilograms, the predicted distance is 16.33 meters. On the scatter plot, that's near the center of a vertical strip around 100 kilograms. Here are the predictions for all the values of Weight Lifted. You can see that they go through the center of the scatter plot, to a rough approximation. Note: We fit a quadratic here because it was suggested in the original study. But it is worth noting that at the rightmost end of the graph, the quadratic curve appears to be close to peaking, after which the curve will start going downwards. So we might not want to use this model for new athletes who can lift weights much hig

her than those in our data set.

title: The Central Limit Theorem

questions.question: What is the probability distribution for the net gain on one bet when playing red in roulette?
questions.answer: The net gain on one bet when playing red in roulette follows a specific probability distribution where there is an 18/38 chance of making $1 and a 20/38 chance of making another $1.

questions.question: How can the net winnings from multiple bets on roulette be approximated?
questions.answer: When betting multiple times on roulette, the net winnings can be approximated using simulation rather than listing all possible values, which involves complex math.

questions.question: What is the result of simulating net gains from 400 spins of the roulette wheel?
questions.answer: Simulating net gains from 400 spins of the roulette wheel results in a roughly bell-shaped histogram, despite the original distribution not being bell-shaped.

questions.question: What does the distribution of net winnings from multiple bets on red in roulette show?
questions.answer: The distribution of net winnings from multiple bets on red in roulette is centered around $20, showing a typical behavior of a simulated probability distribution.

questions.question: How can probability distributions be studied?
questions.answer: Probability distributions can be studied through simulations, which allow us to understand complex random processes without requiring full mathematical calculations.

questions.question: What does the standard deviation (SD) of the distribution of net gains roughly represent?
questions.answer: The standard deviation (SD) of the distribution of net gains is roughly the distance from the center to a point of inflection on a bell-shaped curve.

questions.question: What statistical measure relates to the spread of the net gain distribution?
questions.answer: The spread of the net gain distribution is related to the standard deviation, which measures variability around the average.

questions.question: What does it mean when the distribution of flight delays has a long right-hand tail?
questions.answer: The distribution of flight delays has a long right-hand tail, meaning that there are a few flights with very long delays.

questions.question: What does a standard deviation of about 39.5 minutes indicate about the flight delay times?
questions.answer: The standard deviation (SD) of the flight delays was about 39.5 minutes, indicating a wide spread in the delay times.

questions.question: What result would a sample of 400 delays taken randomly with replacement from the population yield?
questions.answer: A sample of 400 delays taken randomly with replacement from the population would yield an average delay close to the population mean of 16.6 minutes.

questions.question: What is the effect of sampling without replacement from a large population on the average delays?
questions.answer: Sampling without replacement from a large population like 13,825 flights very slightly alters the population, resulting in similar average delays as sampling with replacement.

questions.question: How does simulating the sampling process repeatedly help regarding the sample average of delays?
questions.answer: The sample average of delays varies depending on the sample, and simulating the sampling process repeatedly helps approximate the probability distribution of the sample average.

questions.question: What does the empirical histogram of the sample average approximate, and what shape does it often show?
questions.answer: The empirical histogram of the sample average approximates the probability histogram, often showing a bell shape.

questions.question: What explains why the sample average histogram tends to have a bell shape from skewed distributions?
questions.answer: The Central Limit Theorem explains why the sample average histogram tends to have a bell shape, even from skewed distributions.

questions.question: What does the Central Limit Theorem state about the distribution of the sum or average of a large random sample?
questions.answer: The Central Limit Theorem states that the distribution of the sum or average of a large random sample tends to be normal.

questions.question: What does the Central Limit Theorem state regarding the sampling distribution of the sample mean?
questions.answer: The Central Limit Theorem states that the sampling distribution of the sample mean will be approximately normal, even if the population distribution is not, provided the sample size is large enough.

questions.question: Why is the Central Limit Theorem crucial in statistical inference?
questions.answer: The Central Limit Theorem is crucial in statistical inference because it allows us to make predictions about population parameters using sample data, even when we do not know the population distribution.

questions.question: Why is sampling with replacement important for the assumptions of the Central Limit Theorem?
questions.answer: Sampling with replacement allows each member of the population to be chosen more than once, which is essential for the assumptions of the Central Limit Theorem to hold.

questions.question: What does the Central Limit Theorem state regarding the distribution of sample proportions as sample size increases?
questions.answer: The Central Limit Theorem states that the distribution of sample proportions becomes approximately normal as the sample size increases.

questions.question: What happens to the approximation of the normal distribution when the sample size increases from 200 to 800 while keeping the number of simulation repetitions constant?
questions.answer: Increasing the sample size from 200 to 800 while keeping the number of simulation repetitions constant results in a more precise approximation of the normal distribution.

questions.question: What happens to the variability in the sample proportion as sample size increases?
questions.answer: As sample size increases, the variability in the sample proportion decreases. This leads to more tightly clustered proportions around the actual value.

questions.question: How do larger sample sizes affect the variability in statistics?
questions.answer: Larger sample sizes generally lead to reduced variability in statistics, making estimates more precise.

questions.question: What does the lesson explore about sample size and variability?
questions.answer: The lesson explores how sample size quantitatively affects the variability of sample averages or proportions.

content_md:
The Central Limit Theorem Very few of the data histograms that we have seen in this course have been bell shaped. When we have come across a bell shaped distribution, it has almost invariably been an empirical histogram of a statistic based on a random sample. The examples below show two very different situations in which an approximate bell shape appears in such histograms.  Net Gain in Roulette In an earlier section, the bell appeared as the rough shape of the total amount of money we would make if we placed the same bet repeatedly on different spins of a roulette wheel. Recall that the bet on red pays even money, 1 to 1. We defined the function redwinnings that returns the net winnings on one \$1 bet on red. Specifically, the function takes a color as its argument and returns 1 if the color is red. For all other colors it returns 1. The table red shows each pocket's winnings on red. Y

our net gain on one bet is one random draw from the Winnings: Red column. There is an 18/38 chance making \$1, and a 20/38 chance of making \$1. This probability distribution is shown in the histogram below. Now suppose you bet many times on red. Your net winnings will be the sum of many draws made at random with replacement from the distribution above. It will take a bit of math to list all the possible values of your net winnings along with all of their chances. We won't do that; instead, we will approximate the probability distribution by simulation, as we have done all along in this course. The code below simulates your net gain if you bet \$1 on red on 400 different spins of the roulette wheel. That's a roughly bell shaped histogram, even though the distribution we are drawing from is nowhere near bell shaped. Center. The distribution is centered near 20 dollars, roughly. To see why

, note that your winnings will be \$1 on about 18/38 of the bets, and $1 on the remaining 20/38. So your average winnings per dollar bet will be roughly 5.26 cents: So in 400 bets you expect that your net gain will be about \$21: For confirmation, we can compute the mean of the 10,000 simulated net gains: Spread. Run your eye along the curve starting at the center and notice that the point of inflection is near 0. On a bell shaped curve, the SD is the distance from the center to a point of inflection. The center is roughly \$20, which means that the SD of the distribution is around \$20. In the next section we will see where the \$20 comes from. For now, let's confirm our observation by simply calculating the SD of the 10,000 simulated net gains: Summary. The net gain in 400 bets is the sum of the 400 amounts won on each individual bet. The probability distribution of that sum is approxi

mately normal, with an average and an SD that we can approximate.  Average Flight Delay The table united contains data on departure delays of 13,825 United Airlines domestic flights out of San Francisco airport in the summer of 2015. As we have seen before, the distribution of delays has a long righthand tail. The mean delay was about 16.6 minutes and the SD was about 39.5 minutes. Notice how large the SD is, compared to the mean. Those large deviations on the right have an effect, even though they are a very small proportion of the data. Now suppose we sampled 400 delays at random with replacement. You could sample without replacement if you like, but the results would be very similar to withreplacement sampling. If you sample a few hundred out of 13,825 without replacement, you hardly change the population each time you pull out a value. In the sample, what could the average delay be? 

We expect it to be around 16 or 17, because that's the population average; but it is likely to be somewhat off. Let's see what we get by sampling. We'll work with the table delay that only contains the column of delays. The sample average varies according to how the sample comes out, so we will simulate the sampling process repeatedly and draw the empirical histogram of the sample average. That will be an approximation to the probability histogram of the sample average. Once again, we see a rough bell shape, even though we are drawing from a very skewed distribution. The bell is centered somewhere between 16 ad 17, as we expect.  Central Limit Theorem The reason why the bell shape appears in such settings is a remarkable result of probability theory called the Central Limit Theorem. The Central Limit Theorem says that the probability distribution of the sum or average of a large random s

ample drawn with replacement will be roughly normal, regardless of the distribution of the population from which the sample is drawn. As we noted when we were studying Chebychev's bounds, results that can be applied to random samples regardless of the distribution of the population are very powerful, because in data science we rarely know the distribution of the population. The Central Limit Theorem makes it possible to make inferences with very little knowledge about the population, provided we have a large random sample. That is why it is central to the field of statistical inference.  Proportion of Purple Flowers Recall Mendel's probability model for the colors of the flowers of a species of pea plant. The model says that the flower colors of the plants are like draws made at random with replacement from {Purple, Purple, Purple, White}. In a large sample of plants, about what proporti

on will have purple flowers? We would expect the answer to be about 0.75, the proportion purple in the model. And, because proportions are means, the Central Limit Theorem says that the distribution of the sample proportion of purple plants is roughly normal. We can confirm this by simulation. Let's simulate the proportion of purpleflowered plants in a sample of 200 plants. There's that normal curve again, as predicted by the Central Limit Theorem, centered at around 0.75 just as you would expect. How would this distribution change if we increased the sample size? Let's run the code again with a sample size of 800, and collect the results of simulations in the same table in which we collected simulations based on a sample size of 200. We will keep the number of repetitions the same as before so that the two columns have the same length. Both distributions are approximately normal but one

 is narrower than the other. The proportions based on a sample size of 800 are more tightly clustered around 0.75 than those from a sample size of 200. Increasing the sample size has decreased the variability in the sample proportion. This should not be surprising. We have leaned many times on the intuition that a larger sample size generally reduces the variability of a statistic. However, in the case of a sample average, we can quantify the relationship between sample size and variability. Exactly how does the sample size affect the variability of a sample average or proportion? That is the question we will examine in the next section.

title: Percentiles

questions.question: What does a percentile indicate in a data set?
questions.answer: A percentile indicates the value below which a given percentage of observations in a data set fall.

questions.question: What does a score at the 95th percentile represent?
questions.answer: A score at the 95th percentile means that 95% of the scores are at or below this score, and only 5% are higher.

questions.question: What does the median represent in terms of percentiles?
questions.answer: The median is the 50th percentile, meaning that it is the middle value, with an equal number of data points above and below.

questions.question: What can a value represent in a dataset where all values are the same?
questions.answer: In a dataset where all values are the same, that value can represent any percentile because the rank order does not change.

questions.question: What is important to consider about percentiles in datasets with tied values?
questions.answer: Percentiles require careful definition and consideration, especially in datasets with tied values.

questions.question: What is the 80th percentile of a collection defined as?
questions.answer: The 80th percentile of a collection is defined as the smallest value that is at least as large as 80% of all the values in the collection.

questions.question: What can be challenging when determining the correct index for a percentile?
questions.answer: Determining the correct index for a percentile can be challenging when it isn't clear, such as when finding the 87th percentile in a set of 10 values.

questions.question: What do percentiles require in terms of definition and sorting?
questions.answer: Percentiles require a consistent definition that works for all ranks and lists, taking into account the sorting of values.

questions.question: How are percentiles calculated in a sorted list?
questions.answer: Percentiles are calculated by identifying the smallest value that is as large as the desired percentage of other values in a sorted list.

questions.question: What is the 80th percentile in a dataset?
questions.answer: The 80th percentile is the smallest value in a dataset such that 80% of the elements are less than or equal to it.

questions.question: What inputs does the percentile function require?
questions.answer: The percentile function requires two inputs: a rank between 0 and 100, and an array of data. It returns the corresponding percentile value from the array.

questions.question: What is the general definition of a percentile?
questions.answer: The general definition of a percentile is the smallest value in the collection that is at least as large as a given percentage of all the values.

questions.question: How is the 70th percentile in a dataset defined?
questions.answer: The 70th percentile in a dataset is the smallest value with at least 70% of the data being less than or equal to it.

questions.question: How is the 70th percentile determined in an ordered list of 5 elements?
questions.answer: If 70% of 5 elements roughly corresponds to 3.5 elements, the 70th percentile is considered the 4th element in an ordered list.

questions.question: What is a percentile?
questions.answer: A percentile is a value below which a given percentage of observations in a group falls.

questions.question: What is the first step to find the pth percentile?
questions.answer: To find the pth percentile, sort the collection of values in increasing order.

questions.question: How do you calculate the position for the pth percentile?
questions.answer: Calculate the position for the pth percentile by multiplying (p/100) by the number of elements, n.

questions.question: What do you do if the calculated position k is an integer?
questions.answer: If the calculated position k is an integer, the pth percentile is the kth element of the sorted collection.

questions.question: What should you do if the position k is not an integer?
questions.answer: If the position k is not an integer, round k up to the nearest whole number and take that element as the pth percentile.

questions.question: How can the 85th percentile in a data set be identified?
questions.answer: The 85th percentile in a data set can be identified by sorting the data and finding the element at the position equal to 85% of the array length, rounded up to the nearest whole number.

questions.question: What is the first quartile in a numerical data collection?
questions.answer: The first quartile of a numerical data collection is the 25th percentile, which represents the value below which 25% of the data falls.

questions.question: How does array indexing work in Python?
questions.answer: In Python, array indexing starts from 0, so the nth element is accessed at index n-1.

questions.question: What is the second quartile in a data set?
questions.answer: The second quartile, commonly known as the median, is the midpoint of a data set where 50% of the values are below and 50% are above.

questions.question: What does the third quartile represent in a data collection?
questions.answer: The third quartile, or 75th percentile, is the value below which 75% of the data in a collection falls.

questions.question: What is meant by the 'middle 50%' of a data set?
questions.answer: The 'middle 50%' of a data set is the interval between the first quartile (25th percentile) and the third quartile (75th percentile).

content_md:
Percentiles Numerical data can be sorted in increasing or decreasing order. Thus the values of a numerical data set have a rank order. A percentile is the value at a particular rank. For example, if your score on a test is on the 95th percentile, a common interpretation is that only 5% of the scores were higher than yours. The median is the 50th percentile; it is commonly assumed that 50% the values in a data set are above the median. But some care is required in giving percentiles a precise definition that works for all ranks and all lists. To see why, consider an extreme example where all the students in a class score 75 on a test. Then 75 is a natural candidate for the median, but it's not true that 50% of the scores are above 75. Also, 75 is an equally natural candidate for the 95th percentile or the 25th or any other percentile. Ties – that is, equal data values – have to be taken i

nto account when defining percentiles. You also have to be careful about exactly how far up the list to go when the relevant index isn't clear. For example, what should be the 87th percentile of a collection of 10 values? The 8th value of the sorted collection, or the 9th, or somewhere in between? In this section, we will give a definition that works consistently for all ranks and all lists.  A Numerical Example Before giving a general definition of all percentiles, we will define the 80th percentile of a collection of values to be the smallest value in the collection that is at least as large as 80% of all of the values. For example, let's consider the sizes of the five largest continents – Africa, Antarctica, Asia, North America, and South America – rounded to the nearest million square miles. The 80th percentile is the smallest value that is at least as large as 80% of the elements of

 sizes, that is, fourfifths of the five elements. That's 12: The 80th percentile is a value on the list, namely 12. You can see that 80% of the values are less than or equal to it, and that it is the smallest value on the list for which this is true. Analogously, the 70th percentile is the smallest value in the collection that is at least as large as 70% of the elements of sizes. Now 70% of 5 elements is "3.5 elements", so the 70th percentile is the 4th element on the list. That's 12, the same as the 80th percentile for these data.  The percentile function The percentile function takes two arguments: a rank between 0 and 100, and a array. It returns the corresponding percentile of the array.  The General Definition  Let $p$ be a number between 0 and 100. The $p$th percentile of a collection is the smallest value in the collection that is at least as large as p% of all the values. By this

 definition, any percentile between 0 and 100 can be computed for any collection of values, and it is always an element of the collection. In practical terms, suppose there are $n$ elements in the collection. To find the $p$th percentile:  Sort the collection in increasing order.  Find p% of n: $(p/100) \times n$. Call that $k$.  If $k$ is an integer, take the $k$th element of the sorted collection.  If $k$ is not an integer, round it up to the next integer, and take that element of the sorted collection.  Example  The table scoresandsections contains one row for each student in a class of 359 students. The columns are the student's discussion section and midterm score. What was the 85th percentile of the scores? To use the percentile function, create an array scores containing the midterm scores, and find the 85th percentile: According to the percentile function, the 85th percentile was

 22. To check that this is consistent with our new definition, let's apply the definition directly. First, put the scores in increasing order: There are 359 scores in the array. So next, find 85% of 359, which is 305.15. That's not an integer. By our definition, the 85th percentile is the 306th element of sortedscores, which, by Python's indexing convention, is item 305 of the array. That's the same as the answer we got by using percentile. In future, we will just use percentile.  Quartiles The first quartile of a numercial collection is the 25th percentile. The terminology arises from the first quarter. The second quartile is the median, and the third quartile is the 75th percentile. For our scores data, those values are: Distributions of scores are sometimes summarized by the "middle 50%" interval, between the first and third quartiles.
