mcq_questions.hint: Think about how percentiles relate to the distribution of data values.

mcq_questions.prompt: How is the 70th percentile in a dataset defined?
choices: The 70th percentile in a dataset is the smallest value with at least 70% of the data being less than or equal to it.
The 70th percentile in a dataset is the value that exactly 70% of the data equals.
The 70th percentile in a dataset means 70% of the data is greater than this value.
The 70th percentile represents the average value of the upper 30% of the data.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the proportion of data less than or equal to a certain value.

mcq_questions.prompt: How is the 70th percentile determined in an ordered list of 5 elements?
choices: If 70% of 5 elements roughly corresponds to 3.5 elements, the 70th percentile is considered the 4th element in an ordered list.
The 70th percentile is always the 5th element in an ordered list of any size.
The 70th percentile is determined by taking the middle element in the list.
The 70th percentile is always the 2nd element in an ordered list of any size.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what element corresponds to the 70% mark when you order the list.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is a percentile?
choices: A percentile is a value below which a given percentage of observations in a group falls.
A percentile is a measure of how often a statistical event does not occur.
A percentile describes the average value of all observations in a data set.
A percentile indicates the mode of a data set.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how data is divided into parts or sections.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the first step to find the pth percentile?
choices: To find the pth percentile, sort the collection of values in increasing order.
To find the pth percentile, calculate the mean of all values.
To find the pth percentile, remove all outliers from the dataset.
To find the pth percentile, find the p/100 position on the frequency distribution.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how ordering data might help in identifying specific percentile positions.

mcq_questions.prompt: How do you calculate the position for the pth percentile?
choices: Calculate the position for the pth percentile by multiplying (p/100) by the number of elements, n.
Divide the total sum of elements by the percentile, p, to find the position.
Multiply the percentile, p, by 100 and then divide by the number of elements, n.
Add the percentile, p, to the number of elements, n, and divide by 100.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the percentile relates to the total number of elements.

mcq_questions.prompt: What do you do if the calculated position k is an integer?
choices: If the calculated position k is an integer, the pth percentile is the kth element of the sorted collection.
If the calculated position k is an integer, you average the k and k+1 elements of the sorted collection.
If the calculated position k is an integer, you round k to the nearest whole number and select that element.
If the calculated position k is an integer, you discard it and choose the next higher element.
mcq_questions.hint: Remember what you do when k falls exactly on a position in the sorted set.

mcq_questions.prompt: What should you do if the position k is not an integer?
choices: If the position k is not an integer, round k up to the nearest whole number and take that element as the pth percentile.
If the position k is not an integer, round k down to the nearest whole number and take that element as the pth percentile.
If the position k is not an integer, use the average of the nearest elements.
If the position k is not an integer, discard the result and choose a different method.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider rounding methods when dealing with non-integer positions.

mcq_questions.prompt: How can the 85th percentile in a data set be identified?
choices: The 85th percentile in a data set can be identified by sorting the data and finding the element at the position equal to 85% of the array length, rounded up to the nearest whole number.
The 85th percentile is simply the smallest element in the data set.
To find the 85th percentile, calculate the average of the top 15% of the data set.
The 85th percentile is determined by dividing the data set into 85 equal parts and taking the value at the 85th part.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider using sorting and position calculation on the sorted data.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the first quartile in a numerical data collection?
choices: The first quartile of a numerical data collection is the 25th percentile, which represents the value below which 25% of the data falls.
The first quartile is the median value of the data set.
The first quartile is the average of the entire data set.
The first quartile is the 50th percentile, indicating the halfway mark of the data.
mcq_questions.hint: Remember that the first quartile indicates a quarter of the way through the data.

mcq_questions.prompt: How does array indexing work in Python?
choices: In Python, array indexing starts from 0, so the nth element is accessed at index n-1.
In Python, array indexing starts from 1, so the nth element is accessed at index n.
In Python, array indexing starts from 0, but negative indices are not allowed.
In Python, array indexing starts from 1, so the nth element is accessed at index n-1.
mcq_questions.hint: Remember that Python follows zero-based indexing, similar to many programming languages.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the second quartile in a data set?
choices: The second quartile, commonly known as the median, is the midpoint of a data set where 50% of the values are below and 50% are above.
The second quartile is the smallest value in a data set.
The second quartile represents the average of the data set values.
The second quartile is the maximum value in a data set.
mcq_questions.hint: Think of the second quartile as the halfway point.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the third quartile represent in a data collection?
choices: The third quartile, or 75th percentile, is the value below which 75% of the data in a collection falls.
It is the middle value that divides the data collection into two equal halves.
It represents the average of all the data points in a collection.
It is the value below which 50% of the data in a collection falls.
mcq_questions.hint: Remember, quartiles divide the data into four parts.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is meant by the 'middle 50%' of a data set?
choices: The 'middle 50%' of a data set is the interval between the first quartile (25th percentile) and the third quartile (75th percentile).
The 'middle 50%' of a data set is the range between the minimum and maximum values.
The 'middle 50%' of a data set refers to the median value only.
The 'middle 50%' of a data set is the interval between the 10th percentile and the 90th percentile.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about quartiles and which part of the dataset they define.

title: Why the Mean Matters

questions.question: Why is the mean considered a powerful statistic for inference?
questions.answer: The mean is a powerful statistic for inference because its empirical distribution tends to be bell-shaped, even when the population's distribution is unknown.

questions.question: What is a common feature of the sample mean's distribution despite different possible distributions for other statistics?
questions.answer: Despite different possible distributions for statistics like maximum and total variation distance, the sample mean's distribution is often bell-shaped.

questions.question: Why are random sample means heavily used in data science?
questions.answer: Random sample means are heavily used in data science because their distribution is consistently bell-shaped regardless of the population.

questions.question: What is the empirical distribution of the mean of a large random sample used for?
questions.answer: The empirical distribution of the mean of a large random sample is used for making inferences about a population.

questions.question: Why is the sample mean's bell-shaped distribution important for analyzing random samples?
questions.answer: The sample mean's bell-shaped distribution, regardless of population, makes it a reliable statistic for analyzing random samples.

questions.question: What is a measure of central tendency that represents the average of a set of data values?
questions.answer: The mean is a measure of central tendency that represents the average of a set of data values.

questions.question: How does sample size affect the variability of the sample mean?
questions.answer: Larger sample sizes result in less variability of the sample mean, making it a more reliable estimate of the population mean.

questions.question: Why do empirical distributions of random sample means tend to be bell-shaped?
questions.answer: Empirical distributions of random sample means tend to be bell-shaped due to the central limit theorem, which states that the distribution of sample means becomes approximately normal as the sample size increases.

questions.question: How can sample means be used effectively for inference in statistics?
questions.answer: Sample means can be used effectively for inference by providing estimates of population parameters and assessing the reliability of these estimates.

content_md:
Why the Mean Matters In this course we have studied several different statistics, including total variation distance, the maximum, the median, and also the mean. Under clear assumptions about randomness, we have drawn empirical distributions of all of these statistics. Some, like the maximum and the total variation distance, have distributions that are clearly skewed in one direction or the other. But the empirical distribution of the sample mean has almost always turned out close to bellshaped, regardless of the population being studied. If a property of random samples is true regardless of the population, it becomes a powerful tool for inference because we rarely know much about the data in the entire population. The distribution of the mean of a large random sample falls into this category of properties. That is why random sample means are extensively used in data science. In this cha

pter, we will study means and what we can say about them with only minimal assumptions about the underlying populations. Question that we will address include:  What exactly does the mean measure?  How close to the mean are most of the data?  How is the sample size related to the variability of the sample mean?  Why do empirical distributions of random sample means come out bell shaped?  How can we use sample means effectively for inference?

mcq_questions.prompt: Why is the mean considered a powerful statistic for inference?
choices: The mean is a powerful statistic for inference because its empirical distribution tends to be bell-shaped, even when the population's distribution is unknown.
The mean always reflects the median of a population regardless of its distribution.
The mean is unaffected by extreme outliers in a data set.
The mean can only be calculated from data that is normally distributed.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the shape of the distribution when the mean is used.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is a common feature of the sample mean's distribution despite different possible distributions for other statistics?
choices: Despite different possible distributions for statistics like maximum and total variation distance, the sample mean's distribution is often bell-shaped.
The sample mean's distribution often mirrors the distribution of the original data.
The sample mean's distribution is typically skewed to the left.
The sample mean's distribution is influenced heavily by outliers.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the shape often associated with the Central Limit Theorem.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why are random sample means heavily used in data science?
choices: Random sample means are heavily used in data science because their distribution is consistently bell-shaped regardless of the population.
Random sample means provide exact data from the entire population.
Random sample means are only used when the population is normally distributed.
Random sample means eliminate the need for data analysis.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the distribution of sample means behaves with different population distributions.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the empirical distribution of the mean of a large random sample used for?
choices: The empirical distribution of the mean of a large random sample is used for making inferences about a population.
It is used for determining the exact value of each individual sample.
It helps in predicting future outcomes with absolute certainty.
It is used to calculate the mode of the entire dataset precisely.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how sample statistics relate to population parameters.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why is the sample mean's bell-shaped distribution important for analyzing random samples?
choices: The sample mean's bell-shaped distribution, regardless of population, makes it a reliable statistic for analyzing random samples.
It proves that all data distributions are normal.
It eliminates the need for large sample sizes in experiments.
It ensures that data collection methods do not affect outcomes.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the properties of the sample mean when population distribution is unknown.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is a measure of central tendency that represents the average of a set of data values?
choices: The mean is a measure of central tendency that represents the average of a set of data values.
The median is a measure of central tendency that represents the middle value in a list of numbers.
The mode is a measure of central tendency that indicates the most frequently occurring value in a data set.
The range is a measure of dispersion, indicating the difference between the maximum and minimum values in a data set.
mcq_questions.hint: It's the calculation of the total sum divided by the number of data points.

mcq_questions.prompt: How does sample size affect the variability of the sample mean?
choices: Larger sample sizes result in less variability of the sample mean, making it a more reliable estimate of the population mean.
Larger sample sizes result in more variability of the sample mean.
Sample size has no effect on the variability of the sample mean.
Smaller sample sizes result in less variability of the sample mean.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how increasing data points can influence consistency in results.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why do empirical distributions of random sample means tend to be bell-shaped?
choices: Empirical distributions of random sample means tend to be bell-shaped due to the central limit theorem, which states that the distribution of sample means becomes approximately normal as the sample size increases.
Empirical distributions of random sample means tend to be bell-shaped because all datasets eventually show a normal distribution.
Empirical distributions of random sample means tend to be bell-shaped due to the law of large numbers which dictates consistency.
Empirical distributions of random sample means tend to be bell-shaped because their variance always decreases as you gather more samples.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the theorem that describes the behavior of sample means as sample size grows.

mcq_questions.prompt: How can sample means be used effectively for inference in statistics?
choices: Sample means can be used effectively for inference by providing estimates of population parameters and assessing the reliability of these estimates.
Sample means can be used effectively for inference by identifying causal relationships between variables.
Sample means can be used effectively for inference by summarizing data through visual representations.
Sample means can be used effectively for inference by improving data collection methods.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how sample means relate to estimating broader trends within a population.

title: Variability

questions.question: What does the mean represent in a histogram and how is the spread of data around the mean assessed?
questions.answer: In a histogram, the mean represents the balance point, but the data usually spreads out on both sides of the mean. To understand this spread, we measure the variability around the mean.

questions.question: How is variability in a dataset measured?
questions.answer: Variability is measured by assessing how far values in a dataset deviate from the mean, offering insight into the spread of data.

questions.question: How are deviations from the average calculated in a dataset?
questions.answer: Deviations from the average are calculated by subtracting the mean from each value in the dataset, showing how each value differs from the central average.

questions.question: Why is understanding deviations from the mean important?
questions.answer: Understanding deviations from the mean helps to gauge the typical distance of the data points from the center, which is essential for interpreting data spread.

questions.question: What do negative and positive deviations from the average indicate?
questions.answer: Deviations from the average can be negative or positive; negative deviations indicate values below average, and positive deviations indicate values above average.

questions.question: What happens when all deviations from the average are added together?
questions.answer: When all deviations from the average are added together, the positive and negative deviations cancel each other out, resulting in a sum of zero.

questions.question: Why is the mean of the deviations from the average always zero?
questions.answer: The mean of the deviations from the average is always zero because the positive deviations exactly cancel out the negative ones.

questions.question: Why is the mean of deviations not a useful measure of their size?
questions.answer: The mean of deviations is not a useful measure of their size because it is always zero.

questions.question: How can the size of deviations be better understood?
questions.answer: To understand the size of deviations better, it's important to consider their magnitude without regard to their sign (positive or negative).

questions.question: What is variance and what does it provide an idea of?
questions.answer: Variance is the mean of the squared deviations from the mean. It provides an idea of the spread of data but is not on the same scale as the original variable.

questions.question: Why is the square of each deviation taken in variance calculation?
questions.answer: To eliminate the signs of deviations, the square of each deviation is taken. This method is used because it constructs a measure with powerful properties.

questions.question: What is the standard deviation and how does it relate to variance?
questions.answer: The standard deviation is the positive square root of the variance. It is on the same scale as the original data and measures how much the values deviate from the mean.

questions.question: What does the standard deviation measure using the original units of the data?
questions.answer: The standard deviation, abbreviated as SD, provides a measure of the spread or dispersion of a dataset, using the original units of the data.

questions.question: Why can variance be difficult to interpret as a measure of spread?
questions.answer: While variance provides a measure of spread, it can be difficult to interpret because it is in the squared units of the original data.

questions.question: What is standard deviation (SD) defined as?
questions.answer: Standard deviation (SD) is defined as the root mean square of deviations from the average.

questions.question: How do you calculate the standard deviation?
questions.answer: To calculate the standard deviation, follow these steps: compute the average, find deviations from the average, square these deviations, find the mean of these squares, and take the square root of this mean.

questions.question: Which numpy function can you use to compute the standard deviation of values in an array?
questions.answer: You can use the numpy function np.std to compute the standard deviation of values in an array.

questions.question: What does the standard deviation (SD) measure?
questions.answer: The standard deviation (SD) measures how far data points are spread out from the average.

questions.question: What does it indicate if a player's height is several standard deviations away from the average?
questions.answer: A player's height being several standard deviations away from the average indicates a significant deviation from the average.

questions.question: What does the standard deviation (SD) measure in a dataset?
questions.answer: The standard deviation (SD) is a measure of spread that indicates how much the data values deviate from the average. In most datasets, the majority of entries fall within a range described by 'average ± a few SDs'.

questions.question: How is the concept of 'average ± a few SDs' useful in understanding data?
questions.answer: The concept of 'average ± a few SDs' is used to understand where most data points lie in relation to the mean. This range helps identify data points that are typical versus those that are outliers.

questions.question: Why is understanding the spread of data using SD important?
questions.answer: Understanding the spread of data using SD is important because it tells us how closely data points cluster around the mean. This is useful for interpreting how typical a data point is within a given dataset.

questions.question: What does Chebychev's Bounds specify for any distribution?
questions.answer: Chebychev's Bounds provides a mathematical result that specifies the minimum proportion of data points within a certain number of standard deviations from the average for any distribution.

questions.question: What does Chebychev's theorem assert about the proportion of entries within a certain range?
questions.answer: Chebychev's theorem asserts that for any list of data, the proportion of entries within 'average ± z standard deviations' is at least 1 - 1/z^2.

questions.question: How many data points are within ±2 standard deviations from the average according to Chebychev's theorem?
questions.answer: According to Chebychev's theorem, at least 75% (0.75) of data points are within ±2 standard deviations from the average.

questions.question: What proportion of data entries can be expected to lie within ±3 standard deviations from the mean as per Chebychev's Bound?
questions.answer: For a distribution, at least 89% of data entries can be expected to lie within ±3 standard deviations from the mean as per Chebychev's Bound.

questions.question: What does Chebychev's inequality provide regarding the percentage of entries within a certain number of standard deviations from the average?
questions.answer: Chebychev's inequality provides a lower bound for the percentage of entries within a certain number of standard deviations from the average, not an exact answer or an approximation.

questions.question: What does Chebychev's theorem say about the range 'average ± 2 SDs' in terms of data entries?
questions.answer: The range 'average ± 2 SDs' must contain at least 75% of the data entries, according to Chebychev's theorem.

questions.question: What do standard units measure in relation to standard deviations and the average?
questions.answer: Standard units are a measure of how many standard deviations a value is from the average, with negative values indicating values below the average and positive values above.

questions.question: How can a value be converted to standard units?
questions.answer: To convert a value to standard units, calculate the deviation from the average and divide it by the standard deviation.

questions.question: What does Chebychev's bounds suggest about the range of standard units in any distribution?
questions.answer: According to Chebychev's bounds, standard units in any distribution typically fall within the (-5, 5) range.

questions.question: What are standard units and how do they express values?
questions.answer: Standard units are a way of expressing values in terms of how many standard deviations they are from the average.

questions.question: How do you convert an array of numbers to standard units?
questions.answer: To convert an array of numbers to standard units, a function is defined that normalizes each value by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation.

questions.question: What can we compare using standard units?
questions.answer: Using standard units, we can compare the delay times of United Airlines flights alongside their raw values in minutes.

questions.question: What happens to exceptionally large delay times when standardized?
questions.answer: When standardized, delay times that appear exceptionally large in raw data are rare, aligning with expectations from Chebychev's bounds.

questions.question: What does it indicate if the highest value of delay in the dataset is more than 14 standard deviations above the average?
questions.answer: The highest value of delay in the dataset is more than 14 standard deviations above the average, indicating extreme instances where flights can be delayed by almost 10 hours.

questions.question: What does Chebyshev's inequality state regarding data points and standard deviations from the mean?
questions.answer: Chebyshev's inequality states that the proportion of data points within a certain number of standard deviations from the mean is always higher than a specific percentage, regardless of distribution.

questions.question: What do standard units or z-scores measure in data analysis?
questions.answer: Standard units, or z-scores, measure how many standard deviations a particular data point is from the mean.

questions.question: What are histograms?
questions.answer: Histograms are graphical representations of the distribution of numerical data.

questions.question: How does a histogram use bars?
questions.answer: A histogram uses bars to show the frequency of data intervals.

questions.question: In a histogram, what do larger bars represent?
questions.answer: In a histogram, larger bars represent intervals where data points are more frequent.

questions.question: What might not be easily visible in a histogram due to the chosen bin sizes?
questions.answer: Data patterns or anomalies might not be easily visible in a histogram due to the chosen bin sizes.

content_md:
Variability The mean tells us where a histogram balances. But in almost every histogram we have seen, the values spread out on both sides of the mean. How far from the mean can they be? To answer this question, we will develop a measure of variability about the mean. We will start by describing how to calculate the measure. Then we will see why it is a good measure to calculate.  The Rough Size of Deviations from Average For simplicity, we will begin our calculations in the context of a simple array anynumbers consisting of just four values. As you will see, our method will extend easily to any other array of values. The goal is to measure roughly how far off the numbers are from their average. To do this, we first need the average: Next, let's find out how far each value is from the mean. These are called the deviations from the average. A "deviation from average" is just a value minus 

the average. The table calculationsteps displays the results. Some of the deviations are negative; those correspond to values that are below average. Positive deviations correspond to aboveaverage values. To calculate roughly how big the deviations are, it is natural to compute the mean of the deviations. But something interesting happens when all the deviations are added together: The positive deviations exactly cancel out the negative ones. This is true of all lists of numbers, no matter what the histogram of the list looks like: the sum of the deviations from average is zero. Since the sum of the deviations is 0, the mean of the deviations will be 0 as well: Because of this, the mean of the deviations is not a useful measure of the size of the deviations. What we really want to know is roughly how big the deviations are, regardless of whether they are positive or negative. So we need 

a way to eliminate the signs of the deviations. There are two timehonored ways of losing signs: the absolute value, and the square. It turns out that taking the square constructs a measure with extremely powerful properties, some of which we will study in this course. So let's eliminate the signs by squaring all the deviations. Then we will take the mean of the squares: Variance: The mean squared deviation calculated above is called the variance of the values. While the variance does give us an idea of spread, it is not on the same scale as the original variable as its units are the square of the original. This makes interpretation very difficult. So we return to the original scale by taking the positive square root of the variance:  Standard Deviation The quantity that we have just computed is called the standard deviation of the list, and is abbreviated as SD. It measures roughly how f

ar the numbers on the list are from their average. Definition. The SD of a list is defined as the root mean square of deviations from average. That's a mouthful. But read it from right to left and you have the sequence of steps in the calculation. Computation. The five steps described above result in the SD. You can also use the function np.std to compute the SD of values in an array:  Working with the SD To see what we can learn from the SD, let's move to a more interesting dataset than anynumbers. The table nba13 contains data on the players in the National Basketball Association (NBA) in 2013. For each player, the table records the position at which the player usually played, his height in inches, his weight in pounds, and his age in years. Here is a histogram of the players' heights. It is no surprise that NBA players are tall! Their average height is just over 79 inches (6'7"), abou

t 10 inches taller than the average height of men in the United States. About how far off are the players' heights from the average? This is measured by the SD of the heights, which is about 3.45 inches. The towering center Hasheem Thabeet of the Oklahoma City Thunder was the tallest player at a height of 87 inches. Thabeet was about 8 inches above the average height. That's a deviation from average, and it is about 2.3 times the standard deviation: In other words, the height of the tallest player was about 2.3 SDs above average. At 69 inches tall, Isaiah Thomas was one of the two shortest NBA players in 2013. His height was about 2.9 SDs below average. What we have observed is that the tallest and shortest players were both just a few SDs away from the average height. This is an example of why the SD is a useful measure of spread. No matter what the shape of the histogram, the average a

nd the SD together tell you a lot about where the histogram is situated on the number line.  First main reason for measuring spread by the SD Informal statement. In all numerical data sets, the bulk of the entries are within the range "average $\pm$ a few SDs". For now, resist the desire to know exactly what fuzzy words like "bulk" and "few" mean. We wil make them precise later in this section. Let's just examine the statement in the context of some more examples. We have already seen that all of the heights of the NBA players were in the range "average $\pm$ 3 SDs". What about the ages? Here is a histogram of the distribution, along with the mean and SD of the ages. The average age was just over 26 years, and the SD was about 4.3 years. How far off were the ages from the average? Just as we did with the heights, let's look at an example. Juwan Howard was the oldest player, at 40. Howard

's age was about 3.2 SDs above average. What we have observed for the heights and ages is true in great generality. For all lists, the bulk of the entries are no more than 2 or 3 SDs away from the average.  Chebychev's Bounds The Russian mathematician  (18211894) proved a result that makes our rough statements precise. For all lists, and all numbers $z$, the proportion of entries that are in the range "average $\pm z$ SDs" is at least $1  \frac{1}{z^2}$. It is important to note that the result gives a bound, not an exact value or an approximation. What makes the result powerful is that it is true for all lists – all distributions, no matter how irregular. Specifically, it says that for every list:  the proportion in the range "average $\pm$ 2 SDs" is at least 1  1/4  0.75  the proportion in the range "average $\pm$ 3 SDs" is at least 1  1/9 $\approx$ 0.89  the proportion in the range "av

erage $\pm$ 4.5 SDs" is at least 1  1/$\boldsymbol{4.5^2}$ $\approx$ 0.95 As we noted above, Chebychev's result gives a lower bound, not an exact answer or an approximation. For example, the percent of entries in the range "average $\pm 2$ SDs" might be quite a bit larger than 75%. But it cannot be smaller.  Standard units In the calculations above, the quantity $z$ measures standard units, the number of standard deviations above average. Some values of standard units are negative, corresponding to original values that are below average. Other values of standard units are positive. But no matter what the distribution of the list looks like, Chebychev's bounds imply that standard units will typically be in the (5, 5) range. To convert a value to standard units, first find how far it is from average, and then compare that deviation with the standard deviation. $$ z  \frac{\mbox{value }\mbo

x{ average}}{\mbox{SD}} $$ As we will see, standard units are frequently used in data analysis. So it is useful to define a function that converts an array of numbers to standard units.  Example As we saw in an earlier section, the table united contains a column Delay consisting of the departure delay times, in minutes, of over thousands of United Airlines flights in the summer of 2015. We will create a new column called Delay (Standard Units) by applying the function standardunits to the column of delay times. This allows us to see all the delay times in minutes as well as their corresponding values in standard units. The standard units that we can see are consistent with what we expect based on Chebychev's bounds. Most are of quite small size; only one is above 6. But something rather alarming happens when we sort the delay times from highest to lowest. The standard units that we can s

ee are extremely high! What this shows is that it is possible for data to be many SDs above average (and for flights to be delayed by almost 10 hours). The highest value of delay is more than 14 in standard units. However, the proportion of these extreme values is small, and Chebychev's bounds still hold true. For example, let us calculate the percent of delay times that are in the range "average $\pm$ 3 SDs". This is the same as the percent of times for which the standard units are in the range (3, 3). That is about 98%, as computed below, consistent with Chebychev's bound of "at least 89%". The histogram of delay times is shown below, with the horizontal axis in standard units. By the table above, the right hand tail continues all the way out to $z14.27$ standard units (580 minutes). The area of the histogram outside the range $z3$ to $z3$ is about 2%, put together in tiny little bits 

that are mostly invisible in the histogram.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the mean represent in a histogram and how is the spread of data around the mean assessed?
choices: In a histogram, the mean represents the balance point, but the data usually spreads out on both sides of the mean. To understand this spread, we measure the variability around the mean.
The mean in a histogram is the peak point of the data, and spread is assessed by identifying outliers only.
The mean in a histogram is the highest frequency point, and the spread is analyzed by counting the number of clusters.
The mean shows the most frequent value in a histogram, with spread assessed through the range of values.
mcq_questions.hint: Think of the mean as a point of equilibrium for the data.

mcq_questions.prompt: How is variability in a dataset measured?
choices: Variability is measured by assessing how far values in a dataset deviate from the mean, offering insight into the spread of data.
Variability is measured by counting the number of data points in a dataset.
Variability is measured by determining the maximum and minimum values within the dataset.
Variability is measured by calculating the average value of all data points.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how you assess the spread or dispersion of data points from the average.

mcq_questions.prompt: How are deviations from the average calculated in a dataset?
choices: Deviations from the average are calculated by subtracting the mean from each value in the dataset, showing how each value differs from the central average.
Deviations from the average are calculated by adding the mean to each value in the dataset.
Deviations from the average are calculated by multiplying the mean with each value in the dataset.
Deviations from the average are calculated by dividing each value in the dataset by the mean.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the mathematical operation that finds the difference between points.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why is understanding deviations from the mean important?
choices: Understanding deviations from the mean helps to gauge the typical distance of the data points from the center, which is essential for interpreting data spread.
It provides the exact value of the mean.
It helps to determine the mode of the data set.
It identifies the maximum and minimum values in the data set.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how deviations help in describing data variability.

mcq_questions.prompt: What do negative and positive deviations from the average indicate?
choices: Deviations from the average can be negative or positive; negative deviations indicate values below average, and positive deviations indicate values above average.
Deviations from the average indicate how far a value is from zero.
Negative deviations indicate values closer to zero, while positive deviations indicate they are further from zero.
Positive deviations indicate values below average, and negative deviations indicate values above average.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about whether the deviation is above or below the average value.

mcq_questions.prompt: What happens when all deviations from the average are added together?
choices: When all deviations from the average are added together, the positive and negative deviations cancel each other out, resulting in a sum of zero.
The sum becomes twice the average value.
All deviations add to a large positive value.
All deviations add to a large negative value.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how positive and negative deviations interact with each other.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why is the mean of the deviations from the average always zero?
choices: The mean of the deviations from the average is always zero because the positive deviations exactly cancel out the negative ones.
The mean of the deviations from the average is always zero because all data points are equally distributed.
The mean of the deviations from the average is always zero because deviations are always positive.
The mean of the deviations from the average is always zero because the sum of all deviations equals the number of data points.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how deviations above and below the mean relate to each other.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why is the mean of deviations not a useful measure of their size?
choices: The mean of deviations is not a useful measure of their size because it is always zero.
The mean of deviations ignores extreme values in the data set.
The mean of deviations is too complex to compute.
The mean of deviations depends on the sample size.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what happens to the sum of deviations from the mean.

mcq_questions.prompt: How can the size of deviations be better understood?
choices: To understand the size of deviations better, it's important to consider their magnitude without regard to their sign (positive or negative).
To understand the size of deviations better, calculate their average over time.
To understand the size of deviations better, focus on their frequency.
To understand the size of deviations better, look for patterns in their occurrence.
mcq_questions.hint: Focus on the absolute size, not direction.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is variance and what does it provide an idea of?
choices: Variance is the mean of the squared deviations from the mean. It provides an idea of the spread of data but is not on the same scale as the original variable.
Variance is the average of the deviations from the mean and it gives an idea of the central tendency of the data.
Variance is the mean of absolute deviations from the median and it shows how data points concentrate around the median.
Variance is the sum of the deviations from the mean, providing an idea of how data points cluster around the mode.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what variance measures and the relationship between variance and the mean of data.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why is the square of each deviation taken in variance calculation?
choices: To eliminate the signs of deviations, the square of each deviation is taken. This method is used because it constructs a measure with powerful properties.
To ensure the deviations are overestimated for more conservative calculations.
Because it simplifies the process of calculating the mean of deviations.
To transform the deviations into standard units for easier comparison.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about why we need a positive distance and robust properties when calculating variance.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the standard deviation and how does it relate to variance?
choices: The standard deviation is the positive square root of the variance. It is on the same scale as the original data and measures how much the values deviate from the mean.
The standard deviation is the variance multiplied by the mean and represents the spread of data in square units.
The standard deviation is the average of the differences between each data point and the variance.
The standard deviation is the variance divided by the number of observations and shows how data points cluster around the mean.
mcq_questions.hint: Remember that variance is related to standard deviation by a mathematical operation.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the standard deviation measure using the original units of the data?
choices: The standard deviation, abbreviated as SD, provides a measure of the spread or dispersion of a dataset, using the original units of the data.
The standard deviation measures the average value in a dataset using scaled units.
The standard deviation indicates the median of the dataset.
The standard deviation represents the range of the dataset in percentage terms.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what standard deviation reveals about a dataset's variability.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why can variance be difficult to interpret as a measure of spread?
choices: While variance provides a measure of spread, it can be difficult to interpret because it is in the squared units of the original data.
Variance is difficult to interpret because it does not consider all data points.
Variance can be skewed by outliers more than the standard deviation.
Variance does not provide any information about the shape of the data distribution.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the units in which variance is expressed.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is standard deviation (SD) defined as?
choices: Standard deviation (SD) is defined as the root mean square of deviations from the average.
Standard deviation (SD) is defined as the average of all data points in a dataset.
Standard deviation (SD) is defined as the median value of a dataset.
Standard deviation (SD) is defined as the sum of all deviations from the mean.
mcq_questions.hint: It involves measuring the spread of data points around their mean.

mcq_questions.prompt: How do you calculate the standard deviation?
choices: To calculate the standard deviation, follow these steps: compute the average, find deviations from the average, square these deviations, find the mean of these squares, and take the square root of this mean.
To calculate the standard deviation, find the median, subtract it from each data point, average the results, square the difference, and take the cube root.
To calculate the standard deviation, sum all data points, find the median, square all values, and then subtract the smallest value.
To calculate the standard deviation, find the highest and lowest values, subtract the lowest from the highest, and square the result.
mcq_questions.hint: The process involves finding the mean and working with deviations and squares.

mcq_questions.prompt: Which numpy function can you use to compute the standard deviation of values in an array?
choices: np.std
np.var
np.mean
np.median
mcq_questions.hint: The function is abbreviated and starts with 'std'.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the standard deviation (SD) measure?
choices: The standard deviation (SD) measures how far data points are spread out from the average.
The standard deviation (SD) calculates the sum of all data points.
The standard deviation (SD) gives the middle value in a data set.
The standard deviation (SD) indicates the most frequent data point.
mcq_questions.hint: It relates to the dispersion of data points relative to the mean.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does it indicate if a player's height is several standard deviations away from the average?
choices: A player's height being several standard deviations away from the average indicates a significant deviation from the average.
It means the player's height is exactly the same as the average height.
It suggests the player's height is only slightly different from the average.
It implies the player's height is perfectly normal and expected.
mcq_questions.hint: Standard deviations measure how data points differ from the mean.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the standard deviation (SD) measure in a dataset?
choices: The standard deviation (SD) is a measure of spread that indicates how much the data values deviate from the average. In most datasets, the majority of entries fall within a range described by 'average ± a few SDs'.
Standard deviation (SD) measures the central tendency of the data set.
Standard deviation (SD) quantifies the amount of skewness in the data distribution.
Standard deviation (SD) is used to identify the highest and lowest values in a dataset.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how data is spread around the mean.

mcq_questions.prompt: How is the concept of 'average ± a few SDs' useful in understanding data?
choices: The concept of 'average ± a few SDs' is used to understand where most data points lie in relation to the mean. This range helps identify data points that are typical versus those that are outliers.
It is used to calculate the probability of events occurring within a specified range.
This concept helps in determining the exact median of the dataset.
It is primarily used for identifying the mode of the dataset.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how standard deviation helps identify patterns or deviations from the norm.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why is understanding the spread of data using SD important?
choices: Understanding the spread of data using SD is important because it tells us how closely data points cluster around the mean. This is useful for interpreting how typical a data point is within a given dataset.
Understanding the spread of data using SD is important because it allows us to determine the maximum and minimum values in a dataset.
Understanding the spread of data using SD is important because it provides the exact median of the data points.
Understanding the spread of data using SD is important because it helps in identifying the correlation between two different datasets.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how SD relates to the average and variation of data points.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does Chebychev's Bounds specify for any distribution?
choices: Chebychev's Bounds provides a mathematical result that specifies the minimum proportion of data points within a certain number of standard deviations from the average for any distribution.
Chebychev's Bounds states that the mean of any distribution can be approximated by the median of its data points.
Chebychev's Bounds indicates the exact shape and type of distribution of data.
Chebychev's Bounds applies only to normal distributions and defines the standard deviations where at least 95% of data falls.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the variability of data in terms of standard deviations.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does Chebychev's theorem assert about the proportion of entries within a certain range?
choices: Chebychev's theorem asserts that for any list of data, the proportion of entries within 'average ± z standard deviations' is at least 1 - 1/z^2.
Chebychev's theorem states that most data is normally distributed within one standard deviation of the mean.
Chebychev's theorem guarantees that 95% of data lies within two standard deviations from the mean.
Chebychev's theorem applies only to bell-shaped distributions and states most data falls within three standard deviations.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how Chebychev's theorem applies to any distribution.

mcq_questions.prompt: How many data points are within ±2 standard deviations from the average according to Chebychev's theorem?
choices: According to Chebychev's theorem, at least 75% (0.75) of data points are within ±2 standard deviations from the average.
According to Chebychev's theorem, at least 50% (0.5) of data points are within ±2 standard deviations from the average.
According to Chebychev's theorem, at least 90% (0.9) of data points are within ±2 standard deviations from the average.
According to Chebychev's theorem, at least 68% (0.68) of data points are within ±2 standard deviations from the average.
mcq_questions.hint: Chebychev's theorem provides bounds that are less strict compared to the empirical rule.

mcq_questions.prompt: What proportion of data entries can be expected to lie within ±3 standard deviations from the mean as per Chebychev's Bound?
choices: For a distribution, at least 89% of data entries can be expected to lie within ±3 standard deviations from the mean as per Chebychev's Bound.
For a distribution, at least 68% of data entries can be expected to lie within ±3 standard deviations from the mean as per Chebychev's Bound.
For a distribution, at least 95% of data entries can be expected to lie within ±3 standard deviations from the mean as per Chebychev's Bound.
For a distribution, at least 75% of data entries can be expected to lie within ±3 standard deviations from the mean as per Chebychev's Bound.
mcq_questions.hint: Chebychev's Bound guarantees a minimum coverage, not exact percentages.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does Chebychev's inequality provide regarding the percentage of entries within a certain number of standard deviations from the average?
choices: Chebychev's inequality provides a lower bound for the percentage of entries within a certain number of standard deviations from the average, not an exact answer or an approximation.
Chebychev's inequality provides an exact percentage of entries within a certain number of standard deviations from the average.
Chebychev's inequality provides an approximation for the percentage of entries exactly one standard deviation from the average.
Chebychev's inequality states that all entries lie within two standard deviations from the average with no exceptions.
mcq_questions.hint: Remember that Chebychev's inequality does not provide exact values.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does Chebychev's theorem say about the range 'average ± 2 SDs' in terms of data entries?
choices: The range 'average ± 2 SDs' must contain at least 75% of the data entries, according to Chebychev's theorem.
The range 'average ± 2 SDs' must contain at least 50% of the data entries, according to Chebychev's theorem.
The range 'average ± 2 SDs' must contain at least 68% of the data entries, according to Chebychev's theorem.
The range 'average ± 2 SDs' can contain up to 95% of the data entries, according to Chebychev's theorem.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the minimum percentage of data entries Chebychev's theorem guarantees for any distribution.

mcq_questions.prompt: What do standard units measure in relation to standard deviations and the average?
choices: Standard units are a measure of how many standard deviations a value is from the average, with negative values indicating values below the average and positive values above.
Standard units are a measure of the percentage difference from the average.
Standard units indicate whether a value is above or below the median.
Standard units are used to measure the exact value of a data point in relation to the total data set.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the relationship between a data point, the average, and standard deviations.

mcq_questions.prompt: How can a value be converted to standard units?
choices: To convert a value to standard units, calculate the deviation from the average and divide it by the standard deviation.
To convert a value to standard units, multiply it by the average and divide it by the standard deviation.
To convert a value to standard units, add the standard deviation to the average and multiply the result by the value.
To convert a value to standard units, subtract the average from the standard deviation and multiply that by the value.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how deviations from the average are standardized.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does Chebychev's bounds suggest about the range of standard units in any distribution?
choices: According to Chebychev's bounds, standard units in any distribution typically fall within the (-5, 5) range.
Chebychev's bounds suggest that standard units are always within the (-1, 1) range.
Chebychev's bounds state that standard units are only found within the (-3, 3) range.
According to Chebychev's bounds, standard units are confined to the (-2, 2) range.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the flexibility Chebychev's bounds provide for any distribution.

mcq_questions.prompt: What are standard units and how do they express values?
choices: Standard units are a way of expressing values in terms of how many standard deviations they are from the average.
Standard units measure values as percentages of a total.
Standard units represent values in terms of absolute counts.
Standard units are metrics used for converting between different measurement systems.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how values relate to the mean in a standardized form.

mcq_questions.prompt: How do you convert an array of numbers to standard units?
choices: To convert an array of numbers to standard units, a function is defined that normalizes each value by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation.
By multiplying each value in the array by the mean and adding the standard deviation.
By dividing each value in the array by the mean and subtracting the standard deviation.
By adding each value in the array to the mean and then dividing by the standard deviation.
mcq_questions.hint: Remember, you need to standardize the array by centering and scaling.

mcq_questions.prompt: What can we compare using standard units?
choices: Using standard units, we can compare the delay times of United Airlines flights alongside their raw values in minutes.
Using standard units, we can compare the number of flights departing from different airports.
Using standard units, we can compare the popularity of airlines by counting the number of reviews.
Using standard units, we can compare the various qualities of airline food services.
mcq_questions.hint: Standard units help in comparing measurable and quantitative aspects.

mcq_questions.prompt: What happens to exceptionally large delay times when standardized?
choices: When standardized, delay times that appear exceptionally large in raw data are rare, aligning with expectations from Chebychev's bounds.
Standardized delay times become more frequent and deviate significantly from Chebychev's bounds.
Delay times remain exceptionally large even after standardization.
Standardized delay times are exactly the same as in raw data without any changes.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how standardization affects the frequency of extreme values.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does it indicate if the highest value of delay in the dataset is more than 14 standard deviations above the average?
choices: The highest value of delay in the dataset is more than 14 standard deviations above the average, indicating extreme instances where flights can be delayed by almost 10 hours.
The dataset is normally distributed and such a delay is common.
The highest value of delay is below the average, showing minimal delay instances.
Most flights experience a delay close to the average, making this a typical delay.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how unusual it is for a delay to be 14 standard deviations from the average.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does Chebyshev's inequality state regarding data points and standard deviations from the mean?
choices: Chebyshev's inequality states that the proportion of data points within a certain number of standard deviations from the mean is always higher than a specific percentage, regardless of distribution.
Chebyshev's inequality states that data points tend to cluster closely around the mean in a normal distribution.
Chebyshev's inequality claims that the probability of outliers is higher in distributions with larger standard deviations.
Chebyshev's inequality suggests that the mean and median are closer in symmetric distributions.
mcq_questions.hint: This inequality applies to all distributions, not just normal ones.

mcq_questions.prompt: What do standard units or z-scores measure in data analysis?
choices: Standard units, or z-scores, measure how many standard deviations a particular data point is from the mean.
Standard units, or z-scores, indicate the absolute magnitude of a data point.
Standard units, or z-scores, predict the outcome of future data points.
Standard units, or z-scores, calculate the variance within a data set.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the relationship between a data point and the average of a dataset.

mcq_questions.prompt: What are histograms?
choices: Histograms are graphical representations of the distribution of numerical data.
Histograms are charts to display the frequency of categorical data using vertical bars.
Histograms are diagrams that show the relationship between two variables using dots.
Histograms are a set of mathematical formulas used to model data distributions.
mcq_questions.hint: Think of a type of chart that visually shows how often each different value in a set of data occurs.

mcq_questions.prompt: How does a histogram use bars?
choices: A histogram uses bars to show the frequency of data intervals.
A histogram uses bars to represent different categories entirely.
A histogram uses bars to indicate cumulative data over time.
A histogram uses bars to show the relationship between two variables.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how data distribution across ranges is visually represented.

mcq_questions.prompt: In a histogram, what do larger bars represent?
choices: In a histogram, larger bars represent intervals where data points are more frequent.
In a histogram, larger bars represent intervals with fewer data points.
In a histogram, larger bars represent the maximum possible value in a data set.
In a histogram, larger bars represent intervals with no data points.

mcq_questions.prompt: What might not be easily visible in a histogram due to the chosen bin sizes?
choices: Data patterns or anomalies might not be easily visible in a histogram due to the chosen bin sizes.
The exact number of data points in each bin.
The mean value of the data set.
The overall distribution shape.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about what information might be obscured by how data is grouped in a histogram.

title: The Bootstrap

questions.question: What is bootstrap sampling used for in data science?
questions.answer: In bootstrap sampling, data scientists use the data in a random sample to estimate unknown parameters by resampling with replacement.

questions.question: How does the bootstrap method help data scientists estimate variability?
questions.answer: The bootstrap method helps data scientists to estimate the variability of a statistic without needing additional samples from the population.

questions.question: Why might a statistic calculated from a sample be considered just one of many plausible estimates?
questions.answer: A data scientist knows that any statistic calculated from a sample is just one of numerous plausible estimates due to the possibility of multiple random samples.

questions.question: What does the bootstrap technique involve to understand the variability of an estimate?
questions.answer: Bootstrap is a technique that simulates drawing new samples by resampling with replacement from the observed data to understand the variability of an estimate.

questions.question: What does the bootstrap method do by a process called resampling?
questions.answer: The bootstrap method generates new random samples from an original sample by a process called resampling.

questions.question: What does resampling involve?
questions.answer: Resampling involves drawing new samples at random from the original sample.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using the bootstrap method?
questions.answer: The purpose of using the bootstrap method is to perform inference when it is not feasible to generate new samples from the population.

questions.question: What does a random sample entail in terms of selection from a population?
questions.answer: A random sample involves selecting individuals from a larger population in such a way that each individual has an equal chance of being chosen.

questions.question: Why can the median of a sample be used as an estimate for the population median?
questions.answer: The median of a sample can be used as an estimate for the population median, especially with large sample sizes due to the law of averages.

questions.question: What does the law of averages suggest about sample size and population characteristics?
questions.answer: The law of averages suggests that as a sample size increases, the sample's characteristics are more likely to reflect those of the population.

questions.question: What does the variability in sampling estimates highlight?
questions.answer: Different random samples of the same size may yield different estimates for a population parameter, highlighting variability in sampling estimates.

questions.question: What is required to estimate population parameters from samples?
questions.answer: Estimating population parameters from samples requires understanding the variability that can occur due to sampling differences.

questions.question: What is bootstrap when it comes to estimating the variability of a statistic?
questions.answer: Bootstrap is a method used to estimate the variability of a statistic by resampling with replacement from the original sample, which is treated as a stand-in for the population.

questions.question: What does bootstrap involve instead of sampling again from the population to estimate parameters?
questions.answer: Instead of sampling again from the population to estimate parameters, bootstrap involves resampling from the available sample to mimic different samples we might have gotten from the population.

questions.question: On what fact does the bootstrap method rely?
questions.answer: The bootstrap method relies on the fact that a large random sample from the population is likely to resemble the population itself, allowing us to infer population parameters.

questions.question: What does resampling in the bootstrap context mean?
questions.answer: Resampling in the bootstrap context means randomly drawing data points with replacement from the sample we already have, not directly from the population.

questions.question: What is the idea behind bootstrap in gaining insights into the variability of estimates?
questions.answer: The idea behind bootstrap is to gain insights into the variability of our estimates without needing access to the rest of the population data.

questions.question: What is involved in resampling with replacement?
questions.answer: Resampling with replacement involves drawing from a sample multiple times, where each draw is independent and some items may be chosen more than once.

questions.question: Why should the size of the resampled datasets match the original sample size?
questions.answer: The size of the resampled datasets should match the original sample size to accurately reflect the variability in the original sample.

questions.question: How does resampling introduce variation?
questions.answer: Resampling introduces variation because it allows for different combinations of the original data, with some elements possibly repeated and others omitted.

questions.question: Why is sampling with replacement necessary to create new samples?
questions.answer: Sampling with replacement is necessary to create new samples that differ from the original, as sampling without replacement would simply recreate the original sample.

questions.question: What supports the effectiveness of the bootstrap method?
questions.answer: The effectiveness of the bootstrap method is supported by the law of averages, which implies repeated resampling approaches the true distribution.

questions.question: Why are resamples likely to resemble the population?
questions.answer: The original sample is likely to resemble the population, and resamples are likely to resemble the original sample, so resamples are also likely to be similar to the population.

questions.question: What does the sample method in Python do if no sample size is specified?
questions.answer: The sample method in Python draws rows from a table with replacement by default, and if no sample size is specified, it equals the number of rows in the table.

questions.question: What does bootstrapping involve and what is it used for?
questions.answer: Bootstrapping involves resampling with replacement and is used to estimate the distribution of a statistic, such as the median, by repeatedly sampling from an original dataset.

questions.question: What is a bootstrapped median?
questions.answer: A bootstrapped median is an estimate of the population median derived by taking resamples of an original sample and computing the median of each resample.

questions.question: How is the empirical distribution of bootstrapped estimates created?
questions.answer: The empirical distribution of bootstrapped estimates is created by taking many bootstrapped samples and computing the desired statistic for each one, providing insight into the variability of that statistic.

questions.question: What can the empirical histogram of bootstrapped medians be used to understand?
questions.answer: The empirical histogram of bootstrapped medians can be used to understand the variability and estimate the population median.

questions.question: What is the population parameter that the bootstrap method aims to estimate?
questions.answer: The population parameter is the true median of the entire population that we aim to estimate using the bootstrap method.

questions.question: Why may running 5000 bootstrap repetitions take time?
questions.answer: Running 5000 bootstrap repetitions may take time as it involves repeated sampling and calculation of the median for each resample.

questions.question: How is the empirical histogram of sampled medians related to the population median?
questions.answer: The empirical histogram of sampled medians is a result of random draws and can be positioned randomly in relation to the population median, represented by the green dot.

questions.question: What is the goal of the bootstrap process in the context of estimating the population median?
questions.answer: The goal of the bootstrap process is to estimate the population median by generating multiple sampled medians. These estimates should ideally include the true population median.

questions.question: How can you evaluate if estimates capture the parameter with respect to the empirical histogram?
questions.answer: To evaluate whether estimates capture the parameter, it is necessary to recognize if the empirical histogram 'sits firmly' over the true median, meaning it should cover the median within the middle 95% of resampled medians.

questions.question: What does the 'middle 95%' interval of resampled medians refer to?
questions.answer: The 'middle 95%' interval of resampled medians refers to the range within which 95% of the bootstrap sample medians fall.

questions.question: What was captured successfully by the 'middle 95%' interval in the example?
questions.answer: In the given example, the 'middle 95%' interval successfully captured the population median of 135,747 dollars.

questions.question: What does the bootstrap process involve?
questions.answer: The bootstrap process involves taking multiple samples with replacement from an original sample to estimate the variability of a statistic.

questions.question: What can we determine by repeating the bootstrap process 100 times?
questions.answer: By repeating the bootstrap process 100 times, we can determine how often the 'middle 95%' interval includes the true population median.

questions.question: What does the bootstrap method predict about the 'middle 95%' interval?
questions.answer: The bootstrap method predicts that the proportion of times the 'middle 95%' interval captures the true parameter should approximate a certain theoretical probability.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the bootstrapmedian function?
questions.answer: The bootstrapmedian function is designed to generate an array of bootstrapped medians by taking two arguments: the table containing the original random sample and the number of bootstrap samples to draw.

questions.question: How is a for loop used in conjunction with the bootstrapmedian function in this context?
questions.answer: A for loop is utilized to call the bootstrapmedian function 100 times, which allows us to collect the 'middle 95%' of the bootstrapped medians in each iteration.

questions.question: What does performing 100 replications entail in the described process?
questions.answer: Performing 100 replications involves sampling 500 times at random from the original table and generating 5000 bootstrapped samples for each replication.

questions.question: What is obtained from each of the 100 replications of the entire process?
questions.answer: An interval of estimates of the median is obtained from each of the 100 replications of the entire process.

questions.question: What characterizes good intervals when estimating a parameter?
questions.answer: Good intervals are characterized by containing the parameter we are trying to estimate, even though the parameter is typically unknown.

questions.question: What is considered a 'good' confidence interval?
questions.answer: A confidence interval is considered 'good' if it contains the population parameter.

questions.question: Why might confidence intervals overlap on the horizontal axis?
questions.answer: Confidence intervals are displayed on the same horizontal axis, which can cause overlaps because they all estimate the same parameter.

questions.question: How are intervals arranged to prevent overlap on the horizontal axis?
questions.answer: Intervals are stacked vertically to prevent overlap on the horizontal axis, with each level representing a different replication.

questions.question: In a sampling process, how many out of 100 confidence intervals are expected to cover the parameter?
questions.answer: In a sampling process, about 95 out of 100 confidence intervals are expected to cover the parameter, while approximately 5 out of 100 do not.

questions.question: How can the frequency of inaccuracies in sampling methods be quantified?
questions.answer: Sampling methods have the possibility of being inaccurate, but the frequency of such inaccuracies can be quantified using methods based on random sampling.

questions.question: What does the simulation process described involve?
questions.answer: The simulation process described involves drawing a large random sample, bootstrapping that sample to get an estimate, repeating the bootstrap thousands of times, and then selecting the middle 95% interval of all estimates to form a confidence interval.

questions.question: What does it mean when around 5 out of 100 confidence intervals are 'duds'?
questions.answer: Around 5 out of 100 confidence intervals are 'duds,' meaning they do not cover the parameter, which is visible when there is 'daylight' around the green line.

questions.question: What does bootstrapping involve in forming a confidence interval?
questions.answer: Bootstrapping involves repeatedly resampling a dataset with replacement and calculating estimates to form a confidence interval.

questions.question: What happens when a 95% confidence level is used for creating confidence intervals?
questions.answer: When creating confidence intervals, about 95 of 100 intervals will contain the population parameter if a 95% level is used.

questions.question: How do different confidence levels affect confidence intervals?
questions.answer: Confidence intervals can have different confidence levels, such as 80% or 95%, affecting their range.

questions.question: How does the length of an 80% confidence interval compare to a 95% confidence interval with the same sample size?
questions.answer: An 80% confidence interval results in shorter intervals compared to a 95% confidence interval with the same sample size.

questions.question: What proportion of confidence intervals contain the population parameter if the confidence level is set to 80%?
questions.answer: If the confidence level is set to 80%, only about 80% of the intervals will contain the population parameter.

content_md:
The Bootstrap A data scientist is using the data in a random sample to estimate an unknown parameter. She uses the sample to calculate the value of a statistic that she will use as her estimate. Once she has calculated the observed value of her statistic, she could just present it as her estimate and go on her merry way. But she's a data scientist. She knows that her random sample is just one of numerous possible random samples, and thus her estimate is just one of numerous plausible estimates. By how much could those estimates vary? To answer this, it appears as though she needs to draw another sample from the population, and compute a new estimate based on the new sample. But she doesn't have the resources to go back to the population and draw another sample. It looks as though the data scientist is stuck. Fortunately, a brilliant idea called the bootstrap can help her out. Since it is

 not feasible to generate new samples from the population, the bootstrap generates new random samples by a method called resampling: the new samples are drawn at random from the original sample. In this section, we will see how and why the bootstrap works. In the rest of the chapter, we will use the bootstrap for inference.  Employee Compensation in the City of San Francisco  is a website where the City and County of San Francisco make some of their data publicly available. One of the data sets contains compensation data for employees of the City. These include medical professionals at Cityrun hospitals, police officers, fire fighters, transportation workers, elected officials, and all other employees of the City. Compensation data for the calendar year 2019 are in the table sf2019. There is one row for each of over 44,500 employees. There are numerous columns containing information abou

t City departmental affiliation and details of the different parts of the employee's compensation package. Here is the row corresponding to London Breed, the Mayor of San Francisco in 2019. We are going to study the final column, Total Compensation. That's the employee's salary plus the City's contribution towards their retirement and benefit plans. Financial packages in a calendar year can sometimes be hard to understand as they depend on the date of hire, whether the employee is changing jobs within the City, and so on. For example, the lowest values in the Total Compensation column look a little strange. For clarity of interpretation, we will focus our attention on those who had roughly the equivalent of a halftime job or more for the whole year. At a minimum wage of about 15 dollars per hour, and 20 hours per week for 52 weeks, that's a salary of over 15,000 dollars.  Population and 

Parameter Let this table of just over 37,000 rows be our population. Here is a histogram of the total compensations for the employees in this table. While most of the values are below 300,000 dollars, a few are quite a bit higher. For example, the total compensation of the Chief Investment Officer was over 700,000 dollars. That is why the horizontal axis stretches quite far to the right of the visible bars. Suppose the parameter in which we are interested is the median of the total compensations. Since we have the luxury of having all of the data from the population, we can simply calculate the parameter: The median total compensation of all the employees was 135,747 dollars. From a practical perspective, there is no reason for us to draw a sample to estimate this parameter since we simply know its value. But in this section we are going to pretend we don't know the value, and see how we

ll we can estimate it based on a random sample. In later sections, we will come down to earth and work in situations where the parameter is unknown. For now, we are allknowing.  A Random Sample and an Estimate Let us draw a sample of 500 employees at random without replacement, and let the median total compensation of the sampled employees serve as our estimate of the parameter. The sample size is large. By the law of averages, the distribution of the sample resembles that of the population. Consequently the sample median is quite comparable to the population median, though of course it is not exactly the same. So now we have one estimate of the parameter. But had the sample come out differently, the estimate would have had a different value. We would like to be able to quantify the amount by which the estimate could vary across samples. That measure of variability will help us measure h

ow accurately we can estimate the parameter. To see how different the estimate would be if the sample had come out differently, we could just draw another sample from the population. But that would be cheating. We are trying to mimic real life, in which we won't have all the population data at hand. Somehow, we have to get another random sample without sampling again from the population.  The Bootstrap: Resampling from the Sample What we do have is a large random sample from the population. As we know, a large random sample is likely to resemble the population from which it is drawn.
