This observation allows data scientists to lift themselves up by their own bootstraps: the sampling procedure can be replicated by sampling from the sample. Here are the steps of the bootstrap method for generating another random sample that resembles the population:  Treat the original sample as if it were 

the population.  Draw from the sample, at random with replacement, the same number of times as the original sample size. It is important to resample the same number of times as the original sample size. The reason is that the variability of an estimate depends on the size of the sample. Since our original sample consisted of 500 employees, our sample median was based on 500 values. To see how different the sample could have been, we have to compare it to the median of other samples of size 500. If we drew 500 times at random without replacement from our sample of size 500, we would just get the same sample back. By drawing with replacement, we create the possibility for the new samples to be different from the original, because some employees might be drawn more than once and others not at all.  Why the Bootstrap Works Why is this a good idea? By the law of averages, the distribution of 

the original sample is likely to resemble the population, and the distributions of all the "resamples" are likely to resemble the original sample. So the distributions of all the resamples are likely to resemble the population as well.  A Resampled Median Recall that the sample method draws rows from a table with replacement by default, and when it is used without specifying a sample size, by default the sample size equals the number of rows of the table. That's perfect for the bootstrap! Here is one new sample drawn from the original sample, and the corresponding sample median. This value is an estimate of the population median. By resampling again and again, we can get many such estimates, and hence an empirical distribution of the estimates. Let us collect this code and define a function onebootstrapmedian that returns one bootstrapped median of total compensation, based on bootstrapp

ing the original random sample that we called oursample. Run the cell below a few times to see how the bootstrapped medians vary. Remember that each of them is an estimate of the population median.  Bootstrap Empirical Distribution of the Sample Median We can now repeat the bootstrap process multiple times by running a for loop as usual. In each iteration, we will call the function onebootstrapmedian to generate one value of the bootstrapped median based on our original sample oursample. Then we will append the bootstrapped median to the collection array bstrapmedians. Since we are asking for 5000 repetitions, the code might take a while to run. It has a lot of resampling to do! Here is an empirical histogram of the 5000 bootstrapped medians. The green dot is the population parameter: it is the median of the entire population, which is what we are trying to estimate. In this example we h

appen to know its value, but we did not use it in the bootstrap process. It is important to remember that the green dot is fixed: it is 135,747 dollars, the population median. The empirical histogram is the result of random draws, and will be situated randomly relative to the green dot. Remember also that the point of all these computations is to estimate the population median, which is the green dot. Our estimates are all the randomly generated sampled medians whose histogram you see above. We want the set of these estimates to contain the parameter. If it doesn't, then the estimates are off.  Do the Estimates Capture the Parameter? How often does the empirical histogram of the resampled medians sit firmly over the green dot, and not just brush the dot with its tails or not cover it at all? To answer this, we must define "sit firmly". Let's take that to mean "the middle 95% of the resam

pled medians contains the green dot". Here are the two ends of the "middle 95%" interval of resampled medians: The population median of 135,747 dollars is between these two numbers. The interval and the population median are shown on the histogram below. The "middle 95%" interval of estimates captured the parameter in our example. But was that a fluke? To see how frequently the interval contains the parameter, we have to run the entire process over and over again. Specifically, we will replicate the following process 100 times:  Draw an original random sample of size 500 from the population.  Carry out 5000 replications of the bootstrap process and generate the "middle 95%" interval of resampled medians. We will end up with 100 intervals, and count how many of them contain the population median. Spoiler alert: The statistical theory of the bootstrap says that the number should be around 

95. It may be in the low 90s or high 90s, but we don't expect it to be much farther off 95 than that. We will start by writing a function bootstrapmedian that takes two arguments: the name of the table containing the original random sample, and the number of bootstrap samples to draw. It returns an array of bootstrapped medians, one from each bootstrap sample. Now we will write a for loop that calls this function 100 times and collects the "middle 95%" of the bootstrapped medians each time. The cell below will take several minutes to run since it has to perform 100 replications of sampling 500 times at random from the table and generating 5000 bootstrapped samples. For each of the 100 replications of the entire process, we get one interval of estimates of the median. The good intervals are those that contain the parameter we are trying to estimate. Typically the parameter is unknown, but

 in this section we happen to know what the parameter is. How many of the 100 intervals contain the population median? That's the number of intervals where the left end is below the population median and the right end is above. It takes many minutes to construct all the intervals, but try it again if you have the patience. Most likely, about 95 of the 100 intervals will be good ones: they will contain the parameter. It's hard to show you all the intervals on the horizontal axis as they have large overlaps – after all, they are all trying to estimate the same parameter. The graphic below shows each interval on the same axes by stacking them vertically. The vertical axis is simply the number of the replication from which the interval was generated. The green line is where the parameter is. It has a fixed position since the parameter is fixed. Good intervals cover the parameter. There are a

pproximately 95 of these, typically. If an interval doesn't cover the parameter, it's a dud. The duds are the ones where you can see "daylight" around the green line. There are very few of them – about 5 out of 100, typically – but they do happen. Any method based on sampling has the possibility of being off. The beauty of methods based on random sampling is that we can quantify how often they are likely to be off. To summarize what the simulation shows, suppose you are estimating the population median by the following process:  Draw a large random sample from the population.  Bootstrap your random sample and get an estimate from the new random sample.  Repeat the above bootstrap step thousands of times, and get thousands of estimates.  Pick off the "middle 95%" interval of all the estimates. That gives you one interval of estimates. If 99 other people repeat the entire process, starting

 with a new random sample each time, then you will end up with 100 such intervals. About 95 of these 100 intervals will contain the population parameter. In other words, this process of estimation captures the parameter about 95% of the time. You can replace 95% by a different value, as long as it's not 100. Suppose you replace 95% by 80% and keep the sample size fixed at 500. Then your intervals of estimates will be shorter than those we simulated here, because the "middle 80%" is a smaller range than the "middle 95%". If you keep repeating this process, only about 80% of your intervals will contain the parameter.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is bootstrap sampling used for in data science?
choices: In bootstrap sampling, data scientists use the data in a random sample to estimate unknown parameters by resampling with replacement.
Bootstrap sampling is used to organize large datasets for efficient processing through partitioning the data.
Bootstrap sampling is a method for data encryption to secure sensitive information during analysis.
Bootstrap sampling involves creating data visualizations to identify trends and patterns quickly.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how bootstrap sampling may help in making statistical inferences.

mcq_questions.prompt: How does the bootstrap method help data scientists estimate variability?
choices: The bootstrap method helps data scientists to estimate the variability of a statistic without needing additional samples from the population.
The bootstrap method calculates variability by increasing the sample size with new data collection.
The bootstrap method reduces variability by consolidating data points into a single estimate.
The bootstrap method requires knowledge of the population distribution to estimate variability accurately.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the bootstrap method uses resampling to work with existing data.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why might a statistic calculated from a sample be considered just one of many plausible estimates?
choices: A data scientist knows that any statistic calculated from a sample is just one of numerous plausible estimates due to the possibility of multiple random samples.
A statistic calculated from a sample is always the true value of the population parameter.
Statistics from samples are definitive because they include all possible data points.
Statistics from samples are always biased and cannot represent the population accurately.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the concept of different random samples affects statistical estimates.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the bootstrap technique involve to understand the variability of an estimate?
choices: Bootstrap is a technique that simulates drawing new samples by resampling with replacement from the observed data to understand the variability of an estimate.
Bootstrap is a method of organizing data in ascending order to analyze trends and predict future values.
Bootstrap is a programming approach to optimize the speed and efficiency of algorithms without altering their outcomes.
Bootstrap is a financial strategy for minimizing tax liability by redistributing income over multiple years.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how samples can be recreated using the data you already have.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the bootstrap method do by a process called resampling?
choices: The bootstrap method generates new random samples from an original sample by a process called resampling.
The bootstrap method adjusts the size of a dataset to fit a normal distribution.
The bootstrap method accurately predicts population parameters without needing sample data.
The bootstrap method ranks data points in order of magnitude to determine variability.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how the bootstrap method relies on generating new data samples from existing data.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does resampling involve?
choices: Resampling involves drawing new samples at random from the original sample.
Resampling involves sorting the original sample in ascending order.
Resampling involves collecting additional data from new subjects.
Resampling involves reducing the sample size by removing certain data points.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how we might generate variations of an existing dataset.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the purpose of using the bootstrap method?
choices: The purpose of using the bootstrap method is to perform inference when it is not feasible to generate new samples from the population.
The purpose of using the bootstrap method is to increase the sample size by combining multiple datasets.
The purpose of using the bootstrap method is to reduce the dimensionality of the dataset.
The purpose of using the bootstrap method is to optimize algorithm performance on training data.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how the bootstrap method helps in statistical analyses when actual sampling is limited.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does a random sample entail in terms of selection from a population?
choices: A random sample involves selecting individuals from a larger population in such a way that each individual has an equal chance of being chosen.
A random sample involves selecting individuals based on a specific characteristic to ensure diversity.
A random sample means selecting individuals who volunteer from the population.
A random sample involves selecting individuals in a predetermined sequential order.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how randomness affects the likelihood of selection for each individual.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why can the median of a sample be used as an estimate for the population median?
choices: The median of a sample can be used as an estimate for the population median, especially with large sample sizes due to the law of averages.
The median of a sample is always equal to the population median regardless of sample size.
The median is less influenced by outliers than the mean, making any sample size valid.
A small sample size guarantees more accurate estimates of the population median.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about what happens when you increase the sample size.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the law of averages suggest about sample size and population characteristics?
choices: The law of averages suggests that as a sample size increases, the sample's characteristics are more likely to reflect those of the population.
The law of averages states that smaller samples always provide more accurate estimates of a population.
The law of averages implies that sample characteristics diverge from those of the population as the sample size increases.
The law of averages indicates that sample size has no effect on reflecting population characteristics.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what happens to the reliability of data as you gather more of it.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the variability in sampling estimates highlight?
choices: Different random samples of the same size may yield different estimates for a population parameter, highlighting variability in sampling estimates.
Sampling variability ensures that all samples will perfectly estimate the population parameter.
The variability suggests that sample size does not affect estimation accuracy.
It indicates that random sampling will always provide identical estimates.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how different samples from the same population might yield different results.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is required to estimate population parameters from samples?
choices: Estimating population parameters from samples requires understanding the variability that can occur due to sampling differences.
An exact match between the sample and population in every metric.
Gathering the largest sample possible, regardless of methodology.
Assuming the sample perfectly represents the entire population without any variability.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the role of variability in sample representation.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is bootstrap when it comes to estimating the variability of a statistic?
choices: Bootstrap is a method used to estimate the variability of a statistic by resampling with replacement from the original sample, which is treated as a stand-in for the population.
Bootstrap is a method that provides exact calculations of population parameters without using sample data.
Bootstrap is a statistical method used to create new variables in a dataset by combining existing ones.
Bootstrap is a machine learning technique used to optimize the accuracy of predictive models.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how samples can represent populations through resampling.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does bootstrap involve instead of sampling again from the population to estimate parameters?
choices: Instead of sampling again from the population to estimate parameters, bootstrap involves resampling from the available sample to mimic different samples we might have gotten from the population.
Bootstrap involves collecting new samples from the population to verify the accuracy of the parameter estimates.
Bootstrap uses historical data to predict the outcomes of different samples that could be obtained.
Bootstrap focuses on adjusting sample sizes to improve the accuracy of parameter estimation.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how bootstrap makes use of existing data rather than obtaining new participants.

mcq_questions.prompt: On what fact does the bootstrap method rely?
choices: The bootstrap method relies on the fact that a large random sample from the population is likely to resemble the population itself, allowing us to infer population parameters.
The bootstrap method relies on repeated measurements to eliminate all forms of bias.
The bootstrap method relies solely on the assumption that data is normally distributed.
The bootstrap method can be used only with small datasets and does not depend on the sample size.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what a good sample can tell us about the population.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does resampling in the bootstrap context mean?
choices: Resampling in the bootstrap context means randomly drawing data points with replacement from the sample we already have, not directly from the population.
Resampling in the bootstrap context means taking multiple samples from the population without replacement.
Resampling in the bootstrap context means adjusting data points to match the population distribution more closely.
Resampling in the bootstrap context means analyzing a completely new population set to validate results.
mcq_questions.hint: Focus on the 'with replacement' aspect of resampling.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the idea behind bootstrap in gaining insights into the variability of estimates?
choices: The idea behind bootstrap is to gain insights into the variability of our estimates without needing access to the rest of the population data.
The idea behind bootstrap is to use complex algorithms to predict future data points.
The idea behind bootstrap is to minimize error rates by modifying the original dataset.
The idea behind bootstrap is to rely on prior knowledge of population parameters for estimates.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about using sample data repeatedly instead of relying on population data.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is involved in resampling with replacement?
choices: Resampling with replacement involves drawing from a sample multiple times, where each draw is independent and some items may be chosen more than once.
Resampling with replacement involves drawing from a sample once and using the same data multiple times.
Resampling with replacement involves drawing from a population without considering any replacement.
Resampling with replacement involves using a new sample each time without drawing from the original data.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how replacement affects the independence of each draw.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why should the size of the resampled datasets match the original sample size?
choices: The size of the resampled datasets should match the original sample size to accurately reflect the variability in the original sample.
The size of the resampled datasets should be larger to increase the statistical significance.
The resampled datasets should be smaller to save computational resources.
The size of the resampled datasets is arbitrary as long as the results are consistent.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how matching sizes affect the variability and representation of the original data.

mcq_questions.prompt: How does resampling introduce variation?
choices: Resampling introduces variation because it allows for different combinations of the original data, with some elements possibly repeated and others omitted.
Resampling introduces variation by changing the original sample size.
Resampling introduces variation by removing outliers from the data set.
Resampling introduces variation by converting data into a different measurement scale.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how resampling handles the original data and its elements.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why is sampling with replacement necessary to create new samples?
choices: Sampling with replacement is necessary to create new samples that differ from the original, as sampling without replacement would simply recreate the original sample.
Sampling with replacement is needed to ensure that the sample size matches the population size.
Sampling with replacement ensures every sample is identical to the population mean.
Sampling with replacement allows for removing outliers more effectively than without replacement.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how replacement affects the diversity of samples compared to the original.

mcq_questions.prompt: What supports the effectiveness of the bootstrap method?
choices: The effectiveness of the bootstrap method is supported by the law of averages, which implies repeated resampling approaches the true distribution.
The bootstrap method is effective because it always uses a normal distribution for estimates.
Effectiveness is due to the method's ability to work only with small sample sizes.
The method is guaranteed to be accurate because it involves repeated random sampling.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how repeated trials can help in approximating accurate results.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why are resamples likely to resemble the population?
choices: The original sample is likely to resemble the population, and resamples are likely to resemble the original sample, so resamples are also likely to be similar to the population.
Resamples are created using advanced statistical techniques specifically designed to mimic the population perfectly.
The population data is directly used in generating resamples, ensuring they are identical.
Resamples are generated in large numbers, which automatically ensures they represent the population accurately.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the similarity between the original sample and the population affects the resamples.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the sample method in Python do if no sample size is specified?
choices: The sample method in Python draws rows from a table with replacement by default, and if no sample size is specified, it equals the number of rows in the table.
The sample method in Python draws a single random row from the table by default if no sample size is specified.
The sample method in Python will raise an error if no sample size is specified.
The sample method in Python draws rows without replacement, filling half the table by default if no sample size is specified.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how Python deals with unspecified parameters in functions.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does bootstrapping involve and what is it used for?
choices: Bootstrapping involves resampling with replacement and is used to estimate the distribution of a statistic, such as the median, by repeatedly sampling from an original dataset.
Bootstrapping involves using a smaller subset of data to train a model for quick iteration and testing.
Bootstrapping refers to the initialization process in which software loads basic information to start the system.
Bootstrapping involves data augmentation techniques to artificially grow the training dataset.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how bootstrapping uses the concept of resampling.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is a bootstrapped median?
choices: A bootstrapped median is an estimate of the population median derived by taking resamples of an original sample and computing the median of each resample.
A bootstrapped median is a method to find the median of a dataset by rearranging its data points in ascending order.
A bootstrapped median is a statistical measure that uses external validation to establish a dataset's central tendency.
A bootstrapped median involves calculating the standard deviation of medians of several different datasets.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the resampling method used in statistics to estimate population parameters.

mcq_questions.prompt: How is the empirical distribution of bootstrapped estimates created?
choices: The empirical distribution of bootstrapped estimates is created by taking many bootstrapped samples and computing the desired statistic for each one, providing insight into the variability of that statistic.
The empirical distribution is created by collecting all possible samples of data in the population and calculating their mean.
It is made by using a single sample repeatedly to estimate the entire population distribution.
The empirical distribution is formed by randomly selecting data points until each is used once.
mcq_questions.hint: The process involves multiple samples and analyzing a statistic.

mcq_questions.prompt: What can the empirical histogram of bootstrapped medians be used to understand?
choices: The empirical histogram of bootstrapped medians can be used to understand the variability and estimate the population median.
The empirical histogram of bootstrapped medians can be used to calculate the mean of the population data.
The empirical histogram of bootstrapped medians can be used to determine the exact distribution of the sample.
The empirical histogram of bootstrapped medians can be used to find the mode of the distribution.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what statistical measures are frequently estimated using bootstrapping techniques.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the population parameter that the bootstrap method aims to estimate?
choices: The population parameter is the true median of the entire population that we aim to estimate using the bootstrap method.
The population parameter is the standard deviation of a sample.
The population parameter is estimated using random sampling without replacement.
The population parameter is the mean difference between two samples.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about a central value that the bootstrap method helps to approximate.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why may running 5000 bootstrap repetitions take time?
choices: Running 5000 bootstrap repetitions may take time as it involves repeated sampling and calculation of the median for each resample.
Running 5000 bootstrap repetitions takes time due to the need to create complex visualizations.
Running 5000 bootstrap repetitions is time-consuming because it requires manual intervention between samples.
Running 5000 bootstrap repetitions takes time due to data being transmitted over a slow network.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the repetition and the computational effort involved in processing each sample.

mcq_questions.prompt: How is the empirical histogram of sampled medians related to the population median?
choices: The empirical histogram of sampled medians is a result of random draws and can be positioned randomly in relation to the population median, represented by the green dot.
The empirical histogram of sampled medians is always centered on the population median.
The empirical histogram of sampled medians is always to the right of the population median.
The empirical histogram of sampled medians shows no relation to the population median.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the randomness of the sample may affect the positioning of medians.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the goal of the bootstrap process in the context of estimating the population median?
choices: The goal of the bootstrap process is to estimate the population median by generating multiple sampled medians. These estimates should ideally include the true population median.
The goal of the bootstrap process is to determine the exact value of the population median through repeated sampling.
The goal of the bootstrap process is to establish a fixed median value that remains constant across samples.
The goal of the bootstrap process is to eliminate variability in median estimates across different samples.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how multiple samples can provide a distribution around the true median.

mcq_questions.prompt: How can you evaluate if estimates capture the parameter with respect to the empirical histogram?
choices: To evaluate whether estimates capture the parameter, it is necessary to recognize if the empirical histogram 'sits firmly' over the true median, meaning it should cover the median within the middle 95% of resampled medians.
Check if the empirical histogram has the same number of bins as the theoretical distribution.
Ensure the empirical histogram aligns perfectly with the theoretical mean across all samples.
Verify that the empirical histogram forms a symmetrical pattern around the entire range of data.
mcq_questions.hint: Focus on the relationship between the empirical histogram and the median.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the 'middle 95%' interval of resampled medians refer to?
choices: The 'middle 95%' interval of resampled medians refers to the range within which 95% of the bootstrap sample medians fall.
The 'middle 95%' interval refers to the range within which the original dataset's medians fall.
The 'middle 95%' interval of resampled medians refers to the range within which 95% of population medians fall.
The 'middle 95%' interval represents the range within which 95% of the original sample measurements fall.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the concept of a confidence interval used in statistical resampling.

mcq_questions.prompt: What was captured successfully by the 'middle 95%' interval in the example?
choices: In the given example, the 'middle 95%' interval successfully captured the population median of 135,747 dollars.
In the given example, the 'middle 95%' interval successfully captured the population mean of 140,000 dollars.
In the given example, the 'middle 95%' interval successfully captured the variance of 2,500 dollars.
In the given example, the 'middle 95%' interval successfully captured the mode of 120,000 dollars.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what the 'middle 95%' interval is typically used to estimate.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the bootstrap process involve?
choices: The bootstrap process involves taking multiple samples with replacement from an original sample to estimate the variability of a statistic.
The bootstrap process involves loading the operating system onto the computer from a powered-off state.
The bootstrap process is about building a website using a CSS framework.
The bootstrap process involves initializing a computer network through a series of diagnostic checks.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how statistical methods estimate uncertainty.

mcq_questions.prompt: What can we determine by repeating the bootstrap process 100 times?
choices: By repeating the bootstrap process 100 times, we can determine how often the 'middle 95%' interval includes the true population median.
We can determine the exact sample mean each time the bootstrap process is repeated.
By repeating the bootstrap process 100 times, we find the true standard deviation of the sample data.
We can calculate the average of the 95% confidence intervals obtained from the samples.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what repeating the process helps us infer about variability and coverage.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the bootstrap method predict about the 'middle 95%' interval?
choices: The bootstrap method predicts that the proportion of times the 'middle 95%' interval captures the true parameter should approximate a certain theoretical probability.
The bootstrap method predicts that the 'middle 95%' interval will always capture the true parameter.
The bootstrap method predicts that the 'middle 95%' interval is the largest possible range for the true parameter.
The bootstrap method predicts that the 'middle 95%' interval determines the mean of the dataset.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about confidence intervals and their coverage probability.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is the purpose of the bootstrapmedian function?
choices: The bootstrapmedian function is designed to generate an array of bootstrapped medians by taking two arguments: the table containing the original random sample and the number of bootstrap samples to draw.
The bootstrapmedian function calculates the standard deviation of a dataset.
The bootstrapmedian function is used to sort a list of numbers in ascending order.
The bootstrapmedian function evaluates the mean value of a dataset.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what type of statistic 'median' refers to and how bootstrapping is used in statistics.

mcq_questions.prompt: How is a for loop used in conjunction with the bootstrapmedian function in this context?
choices: A for loop is utilized to call the bootstrapmedian function 100 times, which allows us to collect the 'middle 95%' of the bootstrapped medians in each iteration.
A for loop is utilized to iterate over the elements of an array and calculate the mean using the bootstrapmedian function.
A for loop is used to initialize variables needed for the bootstrapmedian function before it runs.
A for loop is employed to continuously call the bootstrapmedian function until it converges on a single median value.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how repetition helps in statistical sampling.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does performing 100 replications entail in the described process?
choices: Performing 100 replications involves sampling 500 times at random from the original table and generating 5000 bootstrapped samples for each replication.
Performing 100 replications means repeating the experiment 100 times with the same conditions each time.
Performing 100 replications involves 100 iterations of a simulation with varied parameters each time.
Performing 100 replications refers to conducting 100 individual studies and averaging their results.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the steps involved in bootstrapping in statistical analysis.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is obtained from each of the 100 replications of the entire process?
choices: An interval of estimates of the median is obtained from each of the 100 replications of the entire process.
A single estimate of the mean is obtained from each of the 100 replications of the entire process.
A detailed distribution comparison is obtained from each of the 100 replications of the entire process.
A graphical plot of variances is obtained from each of the 100 replications of the entire process.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what type of statistical information an interval provides.

mcq_questions.prompt: What characterizes good intervals when estimating a parameter?
choices: Good intervals are characterized by containing the parameter we are trying to estimate, even though the parameter is typically unknown.
Good intervals are short and do not contain the parameter.
Good intervals always provide exact values of the parameter.
Good intervals are characterized by being symmetrical around zero.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the purpose of an interval estimate in relation to the parameter.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is considered a 'good' confidence interval?
choices: A confidence interval is considered 'good' if it contains the population parameter.
A confidence interval is 'good' if its range is as wide as possible.
A 'good' confidence interval is one that is centered around zero.
A 'good' confidence interval contains only sample statistics.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the relationship between the interval and the true value it aims to estimate.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why might confidence intervals overlap on the horizontal axis?
choices: Confidence intervals are displayed on the same horizontal axis, which can cause overlaps because they all estimate the same parameter.
Confidence intervals are calculated from different datasets, leading to potential overlap on the axis.
Confidence intervals always overlap to ensure more accuracy in predictions.
Confidence intervals overlap due to errors in parameter estimation.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how confidence intervals are represented visually when they measure the same parameter.

mcq_questions.prompt: How are intervals arranged to prevent overlap on the horizontal axis?
choices: Intervals are stacked vertically to prevent overlap on the horizontal axis, with each level representing a different replication.
Intervals are placed diagonally to maximize spacing on the horizontal axis.
Intervals are arranged in a circular fashion, wrapping around the axis.
Intervals are condensed into a single line to minimize space usage.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how to separate items so they don't touch each other horizontally.

mcq_questions.prompt: In a sampling process, how many out of 100 confidence intervals are expected to cover the parameter?
choices: In a sampling process, about 95 out of 100 confidence intervals are expected to cover the parameter, while approximately 5 out of 100 do not.
In a sampling process, about 85 out of 100 confidence intervals are expected to cover the parameter, with 15 not covering it.
In a sampling process, 50 out of 100 confidence intervals are expected to cover the parameter.
In a sampling process, 100 out of 100 confidence intervals are expected to cover the parameter.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the typical confidence level used in statistics.

mcq_questions.prompt: How can the frequency of inaccuracies in sampling methods be quantified?
choices: Sampling methods have the possibility of being inaccurate, but the frequency of such inaccuracies can be quantified using methods based on random sampling.
Inaccuracies in sampling methods can be quantified by estimating the average sample size.
You can quantify inaccuracies by comparing the sample data to known population data under controlled environments.
The frequency of inaccuracies can be quantified by analyzing the historical data outcomes from different sampling techniques.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider techniques that allow for measurement of reliability in samples.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does the simulation process described involve?
choices: The simulation process described involves drawing a large random sample, bootstrapping that sample to get an estimate, repeating the bootstrap thousands of times, and then selecting the middle 95% interval of all estimates to form a confidence interval.
The simulation process described involves drawing a single random sample and using that to form a conclusion.
The simulation process described involves creating a theoretical model without using random samples.
The simulation process described involves using a small sample to generate a deterministic outcome.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the steps involved in creating a confidence interval using repeated sampling methods.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does it mean when around 5 out of 100 confidence intervals are 'duds'?
choices: Around 5 out of 100 confidence intervals are 'duds,' meaning they do not cover the parameter, which is visible when there is 'daylight' around the green line.
Around 5 out of 100 confidence intervals are 'duds,' meaning they incorrectly estimate the mean of the data.
Around 5 out of 100 confidence intervals are 'duds,' meaning they provide a range that is too wide to be of any practical use.
Around 5 out of 100 confidence intervals are 'duds,' meaning they are computed from incorrect data assumptions.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what a 'dud' interval does not do regarding the parameter it aims to estimate.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does bootstrapping involve in forming a confidence interval?
choices: Bootstrapping involves repeatedly resampling a dataset with replacement and calculating estimates to form a confidence interval.
Bootstrapping involves directly using the entire dataset without resampling to calculate estimates.
Bootstrapping involves using only a single resampling of the dataset to form a confidence interval.
Bootstrapping involves using external datasets to calculate estimates for a confidence interval.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the method of using repeated samples to approximate statistics.

mcq_questions.prompt: What happens when a 95% confidence level is used for creating confidence intervals?
choices: When creating confidence intervals, about 95 of 100 intervals will contain the population parameter if a 95% level is used.
A larger sample size is not needed for a 95% confidence level.
The confidence interval will always be narrower than a 90% confidence level.
The 95% confidence level guarantees that the sample mean equals the population mean.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the meaning of a 95% confidence level in terms of repeated sampling.

mcq_questions.prompt: How do different confidence levels affect confidence intervals?
choices: Confidence intervals can have different confidence levels, such as 80% or 95%, affecting their range.
Confidence levels do not affect the range of confidence intervals at all.
Higher confidence levels make confidence intervals narrower.
Lower confidence levels result in wider confidence intervals.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how increasing or decreasing the confidence level might change the interval's certainty.

mcq_questions.prompt: How does the length of an 80% confidence interval compare to a 95% confidence interval with the same sample size?
choices: An 80% confidence interval results in shorter intervals compared to a 95% confidence interval with the same sample size.
An 80% confidence interval results in longer intervals compared to a 95% confidence interval with the same sample size.
An 80% confidence interval has the same length as a 95% confidence interval with the same sample size.
An 80% confidence interval is unrelated in length to a 95% confidence interval with the same sample size.
mcq_questions.hint: Lower confidence levels result in narrower intervals.

mcq_questions.prompt: What proportion of confidence intervals contain the population parameter if the confidence level is set to 80%?
choices: If the confidence level is set to 80%, only about 80% of the intervals will contain the population parameter.
If the confidence level is set to 80%, about 95% of the intervals will contain the population parameter.
If the confidence level is set to 80%, none of the intervals will contain the population parameter.
If the confidence level is set to 80%, about 50% of the intervals will contain the population parameter.
mcq_questions.hint: The confidence level percentage directly indicates the proportion of confidence intervals that contain the population parameter.

title: Using Confidence Intervals

questions.question: What does a confidence interval estimate based on a random sample of data?
questions.answer: A confidence interval estimates an unknown parameter based on a random sample of data.

questions.question: What does a 95% confidence interval imply about the correctness of our estimation process?
questions.answer: A 95% confidence interval means our estimation process is correct about 95% of the time.

questions.question: Should confidence intervals be used to make claims about the percentage of the sample within the interval range?
questions.answer: Confidence intervals should not be used to make claims that a certain percentage of the sample falls within the interval range.

questions.question: Do confidence intervals provide the probability that the true parameter is within the specified range?
questions.answer: Confidence intervals do not provide the probability that the true parameter lies within the specified range.

questions.question: What do confidence intervals provide an estimate for?
questions.answer: Confidence intervals provide an estimate for a parameter, such as the average of all ages in a population.

questions.question: What are some uses of confidence intervals beyond estimating parameters?
questions.answer: Confidence intervals can have uses beyond just estimating parameters, such as testing hypotheses about population parameters.

questions.question: What does a histogram of sampled ages show about the data?
questions.answer: A histogram of sampled ages shows that there can be quite a bit of variation in the data.

questions.question: What is true about the percentage of data points in a sample within a specific confidence interval?
questions.answer: A small percentage of data points in the sample may fall within a specific confidence interval, similar to what is expected in the population.

questions.question: What does a 95% confidence interval mean in terms of the true population mean?
questions.answer: A 95% confidence interval means that there is a 95% chance that the true population mean falls within this interval.

questions.question: What decision is made in hypothesis testing if the null hypothesis value is not in the confidence interval?
questions.answer: In hypothesis testing, if the value specified in the null hypothesis is not in the confidence interval, you reject the null hypothesis at the chosen level of significance.

questions.question: How does the duality between confidence intervals and hypothesis tests help us make decisions regarding the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: The duality between confidence intervals and hypothesis tests allows us to use confidence intervals to decide whether to reject the null hypothesis.

questions.question: What does a p-value cutoff of 5% imply when using confidence intervals?
questions.answer: A p-value cutoff of 5% corresponds to rejecting the null hypothesis if the value tested is not within the 95% confidence interval.

questions.question: Why does using a 1% p-value cutoff require a more stringent criterion for rejecting the null hypothesis compared to a 5% cutoff?
questions.answer: Using a 1% p-value cutoff would require a more stringent criterion for rejecting the null hypothesis compared to a 5% cutoff.

questions.question: What is a 99% confidence interval used for in relation to the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: A 99% confidence interval is used to check if a specified value in the null hypothesis lies within it, which can apply to population means and large sample proportions.

questions.question: How does a 99% confidence interval relate to hypothesis testing and the null hypothesis?
questions.answer: For hypothesis testing, a 99% confidence interval provides a method to determine if the null hypothesis value is plausible for the population mean.

questions.question: How can hypothesis tests be useful when testing if an average age equals a specific value in a population?
questions.answer: Testing if an average age equals 30 in a population using a confidence interval may seem unclear in value, but hypothesis tests can be useful in specific situations.

questions.question: How are hypothesis tests used in randomized controlled trials for treatments?
questions.answer: In randomized controlled trials, hypothesis tests can compare baseline and post-treatment scores, like in studies on Hodgkin's disease treatments.

questions.question: What factors can lead to a wider confidence interval?
questions.answer: A wider confidence interval may result from a high confidence level, such as 99%, or a small sample size.

questions.question: How does sample size affect the accuracy of estimates?
questions.answer: The accuracy of estimates can be influenced by the sample size; larger samples typically lead to more accurate estimates.

questions.question: What does it mean when the distribution of sample means is bell-shaped even if the underlying data distribution is not?
questions.answer: Even if the underlying data distribution is not bell-shaped, the distribution of sample means often is, demonstrating a concept related to the central limit theorem.

questions.question: Why are statistical methods essential in research?
questions.answer: Statistical methods are essential for validating research findings.

content_md:
Using Confidence Intervals A confidence interval has a single purpose – to estimate an unknown parameter based on data in a random sample. In the last section, we said that the interval (36%, 42%) was an approximate 95% confidence interval for the percent of smokers among mothers in the population. That was a formal way of saying that by our estimate, the percent of smokers among the mothers in the population was somewhere between 36% and 42%, and that our process of estimation is correct about 95% of the time. It is important to resist the impulse to use confidence intervals for other purposes. For example, recall that we calculated the interval (26.9 years, 27.6 years) as an approximate 95% confidence interval for the average age of mothers in the population. A dismayingly common misuse of the interval is to conclude that about 95% of the women were between 26.9 years and 27.6 years ol

d. You don't need to know much about confidence intervals to see that this can't be right – you wouldn't expect 95% of mothers to all be within a few months of each other in age. Indeed, the histogram of the sampled ages shows quite a bit of variation. A small percent of the sampled ages are in the (26.9, 27.6) interval, and you would expect a similar small percent in the population. The interval just estimates one number: the average of all the ages in the population. However, estimating a parameter by confidence intervals does have an important use besides just telling us roughly how big the parameter is.  Using a Confidence Interval to Test Hypotheses Our approximate 95% confidence interval for the average age in the population goes from 26.9 years to 27.6 years. Suppose someone wants to test the following hypotheses: Null hypothesis: The average age in the population is 30 years. Alt

ernative hypothesis: The average age in the population is not 30 years. Then, if you were using the 5% cutoff for the pvalue, you would reject the null hypothesis. This is because 30 is not in the 95% confidence interval for the population average. At the 5% level of significance, 30 is not a plausible value for the population average. This use of confidence intervals is the result of a duality between confidence intervals and tests: if you are testing whether or not the population mean is a particular value x, and you use the 5% cutoff for the pvalue, then you will reject the null hypothesis if x is not in your 95% confidence interval for the mean. This can be established by statistical theory. In practice, it just boils down to checking whether or not the value specified in the null hypothesis lies in the confidence interval. If you were using the 1% cutoff for the Pvalue, you would ha

ve to check if the value specified in the null hypothesis lies in a 99% confidence interval for the population mean. To a rough approximation, these statements are also true for population proportions, provided the sample is large. While we now have a way of using confidence intervals to test a particular kind of hypothesis, you might wonder about the value of testing whether or not the average age in a population is equal to 30. Indeed, the value isn't clear. But there are some situations in which a test of this kind of hypothesis is both natural and useful.  Comparing Baseline and PostTreatment Scores We will study this in the context of data that are a subset of the information gathered in a randomized controlled trial about treatments for Hodgkin's disease. Hodgkin's disease is a cancer that typically affects young people. The disease is curable but the treatment can be very harsh. T

he purpose of the trial was to come up with dosage that would cure the cancer but minimize the adverse effects on the patients. This table hodgkins contains data on the effect that the treatment had on the lungs of 22 patients. The columns are:  Height in cm  A measure of radiation to the mantle (neck, chest, under arms)  A measure of chemotherapy  A score of the health of the lungs at baseline, that is, at the start of the treatment; higher scores correspond to more healthy lungs  The same score of the health of the lungs, 15 months after treatment We will compare the baseline and 15month scores. As each row corresponds to one patient, we say that the sample of baseline scores and the sample of 15month scores are paired  they are not just two sets of 22 values each, but 22 pairs of values, one for each patient. At a glance, you can see that the 15month scores tend to be lower than the b

aseline scores – the sampled patients' lungs seem to be doing worse 15 months after the treatment. This is confirmed by the mostly positive values in the column drop, the amount by which the score dropped from baseline to 15 months. In the sample, the average drop is about 28.6. But could this be the result of chance variation? The data are from a random sample. Could it be that in the entire population of patients, the average drop is just 0? To answer this, we can set up two hypotheses: Null hypothesis: In the population, the average drop is 0. Alternative hypothesis: In the population, the average drop is not 0. To test this hypothesis with a 1% cutoff for the pvalue, let's construct an approximate 99% confidence interval for the average drop in the population. The 99% confidence interval for the average drop in the population goes from about 17 to about 40. The interval doesn't conta

in 0. So we reject the null hypothesis. But notice that we have done better than simply concluding that the average drop in the population isn't 0. We have estimated how big the average drop is. That's a more useful result than just saying, "It's not 0." A note on accuracy: Our confidence interval is quite wide, for two main reasons:  The confidence level is high (99%).  The sample size is relatively small compared to those in our earlier examples. In the next chapter, we will examine how the sample size affects accuracy. We will also examine how the empirical distributions of sample means so often come out bell shaped even though the distributions of the underlying data are not bell shaped at all.  Endnote  The terminology of a field usually comes from the leading researchers in that field. , who first proposed the bootstrap technique, used a term that has . Not to be outdone, Chinese s

tatisticians have .

mcq_questions.prompt: What does a confidence interval estimate based on a random sample of data?
choices: A confidence interval estimates an unknown parameter based on a random sample of data.
A confidence interval estimates the median value of a dataset.
A confidence interval determines the range of data values that are outliers.
A confidence interval calculates the average of the sample data.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what unknown value we try to estimate in statistical analysis.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does a 95% confidence interval imply about the correctness of our estimation process?
choices: A 95% confidence interval means our estimation process is correct about 95% of the time.
A 95% confidence interval guarantees that our estimation is within the interval 95% of the time.
A 95% confidence interval implies that 95% of the population data points will fall within the interval.
A 95% confidence interval ensures that the true parameter value is captured within it 95% of the time.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what 'confidence interval' actually measures regarding repeated samples.

mcq_questions.prompt: Should confidence intervals be used to make claims about the percentage of the sample within the interval range?
choices: Confidence intervals should not be used to make claims that a certain percentage of the sample falls within the interval range.
Confidence intervals can accurately predict the percentage of the sample within the interval range.
Confidence intervals are used to estimate the mean of a sample within the interval range.
Confidence intervals directly determine the distribution of individual data points within the interval range.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about the purpose of confidence intervals in statistical inference.

mcq_questions.prompt: Do confidence intervals provide the probability that the true parameter is within the specified range?
choices: Confidence intervals do not provide the probability that the true parameter lies within the specified range.
Confidence intervals give a 100% guarantee that the true parameter is within the specified range.
Confidence intervals indicate that the parameter is most likely outside the range.
Confidence intervals provide the exact location of the true parameter.
mcq_questions.hint: Confidence intervals relate to estimation uncertainty, not probability of exact range.

mcq_questions.prompt: What do confidence intervals provide an estimate for?
choices: Confidence intervals provide an estimate for a parameter, such as the average of all ages in a population.
Confidence intervals provide a measure of variability in a data set.
Confidence intervals provide a point estimate of a specific data point.
Confidence intervals provide an estimate for the range of possible values in a sample.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about what confidence intervals say about data in terms of central tendency or spread.

mcq_questions.prompt: What are some uses of confidence intervals beyond estimating parameters?
choices: Confidence intervals can have uses beyond just estimating parameters, such as testing hypotheses about population parameters.
Confidence intervals are used primarily for graphically representing data distributions.
Confidence intervals can only be used for visual data analysis in charts.
Confidence intervals are mainly for predicting future data points.
confidence intervals can have uses beyond just estimating parameters, such as testing hypotheses about population parameters.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how confidence intervals can help in decision-making beyond mere estimation.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does a histogram of sampled ages show about the data?
choices: A histogram of sampled ages shows that there can be quite a bit of variation in the data.
A histogram of sampled ages shows the average age in the data.
A histogram of sampled ages shows the maximum and minimum ages.
A histogram of sampled ages shows a perfect bell curve.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about what a histogram usually represents regarding data spread.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is true about the percentage of data points in a sample within a specific confidence interval?
choices: A small percentage of data points in the sample may fall within a specific confidence interval, similar to what is expected in the population.
All data points in the sample will fall within a specific confidence interval.
The confidence interval implies most data points in the sample must be outside it.
The percentage of data points in a sample within a specific confidence interval is always the same as the confidence level.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the probabilistic nature of confidence intervals.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does a 95% confidence interval mean in terms of the true population mean?
choices: A 95% confidence interval means that there is a 95% chance that the true population mean falls within this interval.
A 95% confidence interval guarantees the true population mean falls within this interval.
A 95% confidence interval implies the sample mean is exactly 95% of the true population mean.
A 95% confidence interval indicates that 95% of the sample data falls within this interval.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the concept of probability with respect to population parameters.

mcq_questions.prompt: What decision is made in hypothesis testing if the null hypothesis value is not in the confidence interval?
choices: In hypothesis testing, if the value specified in the null hypothesis is not in the confidence interval, you reject the null hypothesis at the chosen level of significance.
You fail to reject the null hypothesis because the result is not statistically significant.
You accept the null hypothesis as the confidence interval suggests no significant difference.
You adjust the confidence interval to include the null hypothesis value.
mcq_questions.hint: Recall the relationship between confidence intervals and hypothesis testing decisions.

mcq_questions.prompt: How does the duality between confidence intervals and hypothesis tests help us make decisions regarding the null hypothesis?
choices: The duality between confidence intervals and hypothesis tests allows us to use confidence intervals to decide whether to reject the null hypothesis.
The duality suggests using multiple hypothesis tests to avoid making decisions regarding the null hypothesis.
Confidence intervals are used to validate the null hypothesis, making the duality irrelevant.
The duality emphasizes using only confidence intervals for proving the null hypothesis true.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how both methods relate to determining the rejection of the null hypothesis.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does a p-value cutoff of 5% imply when using confidence intervals?
choices: A p-value cutoff of 5% corresponds to rejecting the null hypothesis if the value tested is not within the 95% confidence interval.
A p-value cutoff of 5% means that 95% of the data falls within the expected range.
A p-value cutoff of 5% indicates that 5% of the results occur by random chance.
A p-value cutoff of 5% suggests that it is impossible to make errors in hypothesis testing.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider the relationship between confidence intervals and hypothesis testing.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why does using a 1% p-value cutoff require a more stringent criterion for rejecting the null hypothesis compared to a 5% cutoff?
choices: Using a 1% p-value cutoff would require a more stringent criterion for rejecting the null hypothesis compared to a 5% cutoff.
Using a 1% p-value cutoff makes it easier to reject the null hypothesis.
Using a 1% p-value cutoff increases the likelihood of Type I error.
Using a 1% p-value cutoff means you have a wider confidence interval.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how p-value thresholds affect the criteria for rejecting the null hypothesis.

mcq_questions.prompt: What is a 99% confidence interval used for in relation to the null hypothesis?
choices: A 99% confidence interval is used to check if a specified value in the null hypothesis lies within it, which can apply to population means and large sample proportions.
A 99% confidence interval determines the exact value of a parameter in the null hypothesis.
A 99% confidence interval is used to verify if the sample size is adequate for testing.
A 99% confidence interval provides a range where 99% of sample data will fall, regardless of the null hypothesis.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the interval relates to values proposed by the null hypothesis.

mcq_questions.prompt: How does a 99% confidence interval relate to hypothesis testing and the null hypothesis?
choices: For hypothesis testing, a 99% confidence interval provides a method to determine if the null hypothesis value is plausible for the population mean.
A 99% confidence interval can reject the null hypothesis if it contains the sample mean.
The 99% confidence interval always excludes the null hypothesis value.
A 99% confidence interval confirms the null hypothesis if it overlaps with any sample data points.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the confidence interval provides insight into the plausibility of the null hypothesis.

mcq_questions.prompt: How can hypothesis tests be useful when testing if an average age equals a specific value in a population?
choices: Testing if an average age equals 30 in a population using a confidence interval may seem unclear in value, but hypothesis tests can be useful in specific situations.
Hypothesis tests are only useful for identifying trends, not specific values like average age.
They are useful because they eliminate any need for statistical calculations.
Hypothesis tests replace the need for sampling data entirely when determining average age.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how hypothesis tests utilize statistical methods to evaluate specific values.

mcq_questions.prompt: How are hypothesis tests used in randomized controlled trials for treatments?
choices: In randomized controlled trials, hypothesis tests can compare baseline and post-treatment scores, like in studies on Hodgkin's disease treatments.
Hypothesis tests are used to measure the side effects of treatments without any control group in such trials.
In randomized controlled trials, hypothesis tests determine the demographic characteristics of participants.
Hypothesis tests are primarily used to select patients for specific treatment groups randomly.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider what comparisons hypothesis tests are used for in medical studies.

mcq_questions.prompt: What factors can lead to a wider confidence interval?
choices: A wider confidence interval may result from a high confidence level, such as 99%, or a small sample size.
A wider confidence interval is usually due to a low confidence level and a large sample size.
A wider confidence interval is mostly caused by homogeneity in the sample data.
A wider confidence interval results from reducing the variability in your data.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how the size of the sample and the desired confidence level affect interval width.

mcq_questions.prompt: How does sample size affect the accuracy of estimates?
choices: The accuracy of estimates can be influenced by the sample size; larger samples typically lead to more accurate estimates.
Larger samples generally lead to less accurate estimates due to increased variability.
Sample size has no effect on the accuracy of estimates.
Smaller samples usually provide more reliable estimates than larger ones.
mcq_questions.hint: Consider how more data might impact the reliability of your estimate.

mcq_questions.prompt: What does it mean when the distribution of sample means is bell-shaped even if the underlying data distribution is not?
choices: Even if the underlying data distribution is not bell-shaped, the distribution of sample means often is, demonstrating a concept related to the central limit theorem.
The distribution of sample means always matches the shape of the underlying data distribution.
The central limit theorem only applies to large data sets, not affecting smaller samples.
A bell-shaped distribution of sample means indicates errors in data collection.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about a fundamental theorem in statistics that explains how sample means behave.

mcq_questions.prompt: Why are statistical methods essential in research?
choices: Statistical methods are essential for validating research findings.
Statistical methods help in proving the research hypothesis without any doubt.
Statistical methods can completely eliminate errors in data collection.
Statistical methods are only used for analyzing survey data.
mcq_questions.hint: Think about how confidence in research findings is achieved through data analysis.

title: Prediction Intervals

questions.question: What is the primary use of regression in data analysis?
questions.answer: The primary use of regression in data analysis is to make predictions for new individuals based on existing data, estimating a response variable y for a new value of x.

questions.question: What is a fitted value at a given value of x?
questions.answer: A fitted value at a given value of x is the predicted value of y from the regression line at that x.

questions.question: What is the regression line used for when predicting new data points?
questions.answer: The regression line is used as an estimate for the true relationship between variables when predicting new data points.

questions.question: What does a 95% confidence interval for the slope not containing 0 suggest?
questions.answer: A 95% confidence interval for the slope not containing 0 suggests that there is a statistically significant relationship between the variables.

questions.question: What does the fittedvalue function calculate for a given x value?
questions.answer: The fittedvalue function calculates the estimation on the regression line for a given x value, representing the predicted y value.

questions.question: What is the definition of a fitted value in regression analysis?
questions.answer: In regression analysis, the fitted value is the point on the regression line corresponding to a specific input value, indicating the model's prediction.

questions.question: How do fitted values help in predicting outcomes using a regression model?
questions.answer: Fitted values help predict outcomes by using a regression model to provide estimates for specific input values like gestational days.

questions.question: What do you need to compute the fitted value in a regression model?
questions.answer: To compute the fitted value, you need the table, labels of the x and y columns, and a specific x value where the prediction should be made.

questions.question: What is bootstrapping?
questions.answer: Bootstrapping is a technique where new samples are generated by resampling with replacement from the original dataset.

questions.question: How can the variability in predictions be estimated using bootstrap samples?
questions.answer: The variability in predictions can be estimated by generating multiple bootstrap samples and fitting a regression line to each sample.

questions.question: What does each bootstrap sample provide in terms of regression lines and predictions?
questions.answer: Each bootstrap sample provides a different regression line and subsequently a different prediction, highlighting the potential variability in predictions.

questions.question: What does the prediction interval account for using bootstrap samples?
questions.answer: The prediction interval takes into account the variability across different bootstrap samples to provide a range for predictions.

questions.question: What does the bootstrapprediction function generate to create an interval prediction similar to a bootstrap confidence interval?
questions.answer: The bootstrapprediction function generates an empirical histogram of predictions to create an interval prediction similar to a bootstrap confidence interval.

questions.question: What are the five arguments required by the bootstrapprediction function?
questions.answer: The bootstrapprediction function requires five arguments: the table name, the predictor and response variable column labels, the value of x for prediction, and the number of bootstrap repetitions.

questions.question: What does bootstrapprediction do during each repetition to predict the value of y for the given x?
questions.answer: During each repetition, bootstrapprediction bootstraps the original scatter plot and predicts the value of y for the given x using the fittedvalue function.

questions.question: How does bootstrapprediction help visualize the distribution and interval of predictions?
questions.answer: By drawing the empirical histogram of predicted values, bootstrapprediction helps visualize the distribution and interval of predictions.

questions.question: What does a 95% prediction interval represent?
questions.answer: A 95% prediction interval represents the 'middle 95%' of predicted values, calculated as the interval between the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles.

questions.question: How does changing the predictor value affect the bootstrap predictions?
questions.answer: Changing the value of the predictor affects the distribution and results of the bootstrap predictions, as shown by the histogram for 285 gestational days.

questions.question: When is a prediction interval narrower in relation to the mean of the distribution?
questions.answer: A prediction interval is narrower when the predicted value is closer to the mean of the distribution.

questions.question: Where do regression lines based on bootstrap samples tend to be closer to each other?
questions.answer: Regression lines based on bootstrap samples tend to be closer to each other near the center of the distribution of the predictor variable.

questions.question: What do bootstrap replications typically show about prediction intervals and predictor values near the mean?
questions.answer: Bootstrap replications typically show that prediction intervals are narrower when the predictor value is near the mean, due to reduced variability.

questions.question: What should the scatter plot resemble for the regression model to be considered valid?
questions.answer: The predictions and tests performed assume that the regression model is valid, meaning the scatter plot should resemble a straight line with points spread around it due to random noise.

questions.question: What does it indicate if the scatter plot does not appear linear or evenly spread around a line?
questions.answer: If the scatter plot does not appear linear or evenly spread around a line, the regression model might not hold for the data.

questions.question: What should you check before making predictions or testing hypotheses using a regression model?
questions.answer: Before making predictions or testing hypotheses, ensure that the regression model holds by checking if the scatter plot looks roughly linear.

questions.question: What does a valid regression model's scatter plot look like?
questions.answer: For the regression model to be valid, the scatter plot should show points that are on a straight line and pushed off by random normal noise.

questions.question: What may happen if the regression model does not hold?
questions.answer: If the regression model does not hold, calculations and assumptions based on the model may not be valid.

questions.question: What does a residual plot show?
questions.answer: A residual plot shows the difference between the observed and predicted values.

questions.question: What issues in the data can residual plots help identify?
questions.answer: Using residual plots can help identify non-linearity or heteroscedasticity in the data.

questions.question: How can we assess the fit of a regression model?
questions.answer: We can assess the fit of a regression model by examining the pattern of residuals.

questions.question: What does an appropriately random pattern in the residual plot suggest?
questions.answer: An appropriately random pattern in the residual plot suggests a good model fit.

content_md:
Prediction Intervals One of the primary uses of regression is to make predictions for a new individual who was not part of our original sample but is similar to the sampled individuals. In the language of the model, we want to estimate $y$ for a new value of $x$. Our estimate is the height of the true line at $x$. Of course, we don't know the true line. What we have as a substitute is the regression line through our sample of points. The fitted value at a given value of $x$ is the regression estimate of $y$ based on that value of $x$. In other words, the fitted value at a given value of $x$ is the height of the regression line at that $x$. Suppose we try to predict a baby's birth weight based on the number of gestational days. As we saw in the previous section, the data fit the regression model fairly well and a 95% confidence interval for the slope of the true line doesn't contain 0. So

 it seems reasonable to carry out our prediction. The figure below shows where the prediction lies on the regression line. The red line is at $x  300$. The height of the point where the red line hits the regression line is the fitted value at 300 gestational days. The function fittedvalue computes this height. Like the functions correlation, slope, and intercept, its arguments include the name of the table and the labels of the $x$ and $y$ columns. But it also requires a fourth argument, which is the value of $x$ at which the estimate will be made. The fitted value at 300 gestational days is about 129.2 ounces. In other words, for a pregnancy that has a duration of 300 gestational days, our estimate for the baby's weight is about 129.2 ounces.  The Variability of the Prediction We have developed a method making one prediction of a new baby's birth weight based on the number of gestationa

l days, using the data in our sample. But as data scientists, we know that the sample might have been different. Had the sample been different, the regression line would have been different too, and so would our prediction. To see how good our prediction is, we must get a sense of how variable the prediction can be. To do this, we must generate new samples. We can do that by bootstrapping the scatter plot as in the previous section. We will then fit the regression line to the scatter plot in each replication, and make a prediction based on each line. The figure below shows 10 such lines, and the corresponding predicted birth weight at 300 gestational days. The predictions vary from one line to the next. The table below shows the slope and intercept of each of the 10 lines, along with the prediction.  Bootstrap Prediction Interval If we increase the number of repetitions of the resampling

 process, we can generate an empirical histogram of the predictions. This will allow us to create an interval of predictions, using the same percentile method that we used create a bootstrap confidence interval for the slope. Let us define a function called bootstrapprediction to do this. The function takes five arguments:  the name of the table  the column labels of the predictor and response variables, in that order  the value of $x$ at which to make the prediction  the desired number of bootstrap repetitions In each repetition, the function bootstraps the original scatter plot and finds the predicted value of $y$ based on the specified value of $x$. Specifically, it calls the function fittedvalue that we defined earlier in this section to find the fitted value at the specified $x$. Finally, it draws the empirical histogram of all the predicted values, and prints the interval consistin

g of the "middle 95%" of the predicted values. It also prints the predicted value based on the regression line through the original scatter plot. The figure above shows a bootstrap empirical histogram of the predicted birth weight of a baby at 300 gestational days, based on 5,000 repetitions of the bootstrap process. The empirical distribution is roughly normal. An approximate 95% prediction interval of scores has been constructed by taking the "middle 95%" of the predictions, that is, the interval from the 2.5th percentile to the 97.5th percentile of the predictions. The interval ranges from about 127 to about 131. The prediction based on the original sample was about 129, which is close to the center of the interval.  The Effect of Changing the Value of the Predictor The figure below shows the histogram of 5,000 bootstrap predictions at 285 gestational days. The prediction based on the

 original sample is about 122 ounces, and the interval ranges from about 121 ounces to about 123 ounces. Notice that this interval is narrower than the prediction interval at 300 gestational days. Let us investigate the reason for this. The mean number of gestational days is about 279 days: So 285 is nearer to the center of the distribution than 300 is. Typically, the regression lines based on the bootstrap samples are closer to each other near the center of the distribution of the predictor variable. Therefore all of the predicted values are closer together as well. This explains the narrower width of the prediction interval.
